docs.title: Security Model
docs.text: Agent Swarm implements a defense-in-depth security architecture using SPIFFE/SPIRE for workload identity, JWT-ACE for per-request capability tokens, and the MAESTRO framework for layered security controls.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
graph TB
    subgraph Identity["Layer 1: Workload Identity"]
        SPIRE[SPIRE Server]
        SA1[SPIRE Agent · Execution]
        SA2[SPIRE Agent · Turing]
        SPIRE -->|X.509 SVID| SA1
        SPIRE -->|X.509 SVID| SA2
    end

    subgraph Tokens["Layer 2: Capability Tokens"]
        TI[Token Issuer]
        TI -->|JWT-ACE| Agent1[Agent]
        TI -->|JWT-ACE| Agent2[Agent]
    end

    subgraph Validation["Layer 3: Output Validation"]
        V1[AST Parse]
        V2[Coherence Check]
        V3[llama-guard-3]
    end

    subgraph Gating["Layer 4: Capability Gating"]
        CG[Capability Gate]
        GOV[Governance]
        CG -->|blocked| GOV
    end

docs.title: SPIFFE / SPIRE
docs.text: Every service in Agent Swarm has a cryptographic identity.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. SPIRE Server (Control Node, port 8081) is the certificate authority
  2. SPIRE Agents run on Execution and Gateway nodes
  3. Each workload receives an X.509 SVID (SPIFFE Verifiable Identity Document)
  4. Services authenticate via mutual TLS — no passwords or API keys needed

docs.title: Trust Domain
docs.text: spiffe://home-ai-lab

docs.title: SPIFFE IDs
docs.text: Workload SPIFFE ID Execution Node spiffe://home-ai-lab/execution-node Gateway Node spiffe://home-ai-lab/turing-gateway Agent Runtime spiffe://home-ai-lab/agent-runtime

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
• SPIRE Server: control_plane/config/spire/server.conf
  • SPIRE Agent (Execution): execution_plane/config/spire/agent.conf
  • SPIRE Agent (Gateway): turing_gateway/config/spire/agent.conf
  • Key Manager: disk (keys persist across restarts)

Join Tokens

SPIRE join tokens are one-time use. Generate a fresh token from the Control Plane before each agent start.

docs.title: JWT-ACE Tokens
docs.text: Per-request ephemeral capability tokens that scope what each agent can do.

docs.title: Token Lifecycle
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Router
    participant Issuer as Token Issuer
    participant Agent
    participant Gate as Capability Gate

    Router->>Issuer: Issue for intent=CODE, session=abc
    Issuer-->>Router: JWT-ACE {tools: [file_ops, terminal], level: L4}
    Router->>Agent: Execute with token
    Agent->>Gate: Can I use terminal?
    Gate-->>Agent: ✓ Allowed by token
    Agent->>Gate: Can I use mqtt_publish?
    Gate-->>Agent: ✗ Not in token scope

docs.title: Token Contents
docs.text: Claim Description intent The classified intent (CODE, IMAGE, etc.) tools List of allowed tool names level Security level (L1–L7) session_id Conversation session identifier owner_id User identity exp Expiration timestamp

docs.title: Security Levels
docs.text: Level Description Example Intents L1 Read-only, minimal access CONVERSATION L2 Standard user operations CODE, RESEARCH L3 Tool execution allowed DEVOPS, DATA L4 File system access CODE with dev_mode L5 Network operations IOT_CONTROL L6 System-level operations COORDINATE L7 Full administrative access Admin-only

docs.title: MAESTRO Framework
docs.text:
The MAESTRO framework defines 7 security layers. Agent Swarm is 98% compliant.

Layer Domain Status L1 Asset Inventory ✅ Complete — all services cataloged L2 Threat Modeling ✅ Complete — attack surfaces documented L3 Access Control ✅ Complete — SPIFFE + JWT-ACE L4 Input Validation ✅ Complete — schema validation on all endpoints L5 Output Validation ✅ Complete — MarsRL 3-layer verifier L6 Active Defense ✅ Complete — security agent, command blocklist L7 Monitoring ✅ Complete — Langfuse traces, Prometheus alerts

docs.title: Authorization Middleware
docs.text:
The authorization_middleware.py enforces security on every request:

  • Public routes: /, /v1/models, /log — no auth required
  • Protected routes: All others — SPIFFE authentication required
  • Socket path: unix:///var/run/spire/agent.sock

docs.title: Command Blocklist
docs.text:
The security agent maintains a blocklist of dangerous commands:

  • rm -rf /, mkfs, dd if=/dev/zero — filesystem destruction
  • curl | bash, wget | sh — arbitrary code execution
  • chmod 777, chown root — permission escalation

Blocked commands trigger a governance request instead of execution.

docs.title: Audit Logging
docs.text:
All security-relevant events are logged:

  • Token issuance and validation
  • Capability gate decisions (allow/deny)
  • Governance request submissions
  • Security agent blocks
  • SPIFFE attestation events

Logs flow to Loki for aggregation and Langfuse for trace correlation.

docs.text: Document ID: ARCH-DD-004 Domain: Architecture Owner: Architecture Reviewers: Security, Platform Status: Approved Version: 1.0 Last Updated: 2026-03-31 Source of Truth: agents/router.py, agents/registry.py, agents/security_agent.py, agents/corrector_agent.py

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Describe how skill execution and hook processing are orchestrated with policy controls and identity context propagation.

docs.title: Pipeline Stages
docs.text:
1. Router resolves intent and candidate execution path.
  2. Skill registry resolves callable skill/tool handlers.
  3. Security and policy hooks are evaluated before sensitive execution.
  4. Execution is dispatched with request/session/owner context.
  5. Results are post-processed and returned through response safety checks.

docs.title: Security Controls
docs.text:
• Endpoint-class and token-profile enforcement before skill execution.
  • Sensitive operation confirmation requirements for IoT control paths.
  • Structured audit logging for blocked/executed/error outcomes.

docs.title: Observability
docs.text:
• Trace metadata includes session and owner context.
  • IoT-sensitive action counters and alerts provide control visibility.

docs.title: ADR Template
docs.text:
# ADR-NNN: Title

**Status**: Proposed | Accepted | Deprecated | Superseded
**Date**: YYYY-MM

## Context
What is the situation that motivates this decision?

## Decision
What is the change that we're making?

## Consequences
What becomes easier or harder because of this change?

docs.title: ADR-001: JWT-ACE Profiles over RBAC
docs.text: Status: Accepted Date: 2025-12

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Agent Swarm agents need per-request authorization that controls which tools, models, and actions are available. Traditional RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) assigns static permissions to user roles. However, in an AI agent system:

  • The same user may issue requests requiring wildly different permissions (code execution vs. reading a file)
  • Agents act autonomously and need fine-grained, ephemeral permissions
  • Permissions should expire with the request, not persist across sessions
  • The system must be auditable — every capability decision needs a trace

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Use JWT-ACE (Authorization for Constrained Environments) ephemeral capability tokens instead of RBAC.

Each request receives a JWT token that encodes:

  • Intent-specific tools: Only the tools needed for the classified intent
  • Security level: L1–L7 graduated access
  • Expiration: Token dies with the request/session
  • Audit trail: Token is logged in Langfuse traces

The Token Issuer generates tokens based on the Semantic Router's intent classification:

Intent=CODE → tools=[file_ops, terminal, ast_tool], level=L4
Intent=CONVERSATION → tools=[search], level=L1
Intent=IOT_CONTROL → tools=[ha_call_service, ha_turn_on], level=L5

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Least privilege by default: Agents only get what they need for each request
  • No role explosion: No need to define roles for every combination of capabilities
  • Auditability: Every token is traceable; security reviews can see exactly what was permitted
  • Dynamic: Permissions adapt to intent, not static user roles
  • Time-bounded: Tokens expire, reducing blast radius of compromised credentials

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• Complexity: Token issuance adds a step to every request pipeline
  • Latency: ~5ms overhead for token generation and validation
  • Intent dependency: Security depends on correct intent classification — misclassification could over-scope a token
  • Custom implementation: JWT-ACE is not a standard library; we maintain the issuer and validator

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Architecture: Security Model — full security architecture
  • agents/security/token_issuer.py — Token Issuer implementation
  • agents/security/capability_gate.py — Token validation and enforcement

docs.title: ADR-002: Local Hook Execution Model
docs.text: Status: Accepted Date: 2025-12

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Agent Swarm runs across three physical nodes. Code execution (sandboxed environments, ComfyUI workflows, voice synthesis) must run on nodes with GPUs. The system needs a model for how and where tasks execute.

Two approaches were considered:

  1. Cloud-like orchestrator: A centralized scheduler dispatches work to any node (like Kubernetes)
  2. Local hook model: Each node runs its own services; routing is handled by the reverse proxy

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Adopt a local hook execution model where each node is responsible for its own workloads, and Traefik handles routing.

  • Execution Node (Lovelace): All GPU compute — Ollama, ComfyUI, Voice Engine, Agent Runtime
  • Control Node (Hopper): All coordination — SPIRE, databases, Langfuse, MemPalace
  • Gateway Node (Turing): All ingress — Traefik, monitoring stack, secondary Ollama

Services are started via Docker Compose on each node. Inter-node communication uses direct HTTP over the LAN.

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Simplicity: No orchestrator to manage (no Kubernetes, Nomad, or Swarm)
  • Predictable placement: GPU workloads always run where the GPU is
  • Low overhead: No scheduling delay, no container migration
  • Easy debugging: Services are always on the same node; logs are in one place
  • Offline capable: Each node can function independently if others are down

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• No auto-failover: If the Execution Node goes down, GPU workloads stop
  • Manual scaling: Adding capacity requires manual docker-compose changes
  • No load balancing: Single node bottleneck for GPU work (mitigated by queue limits)
  • Tight coupling: Services know their node's IP addresses

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Architecture: Topology — physical node layout
  • Admin: Deployment — per-node setup

docs.title: ADR-003: Filesystem User Storage
docs.text: Status: Accepted Date: 2025-12

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Agent Swarm stores persistent data per user: session histories, learned rules, preferences, generated artifacts (images, 3D models), and project files. Options considered:

  1. Database-only: Store everything in PostgreSQL
  2. Object storage: Use MinIO for all user data
  3. Filesystem: Local filesystem organized by user/project

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Use filesystem-based user storage with Docker volume mounts for persistence.

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
/workspace/
├── agents/
│   ├── skills_memory.json        # Learned rules
│   └── context_sessions/         # Active sessions
├── user_projects/
│   └── {project_name}/          # User project files
├── delivered_artifacts/
│   ├── images/                  # Generated images
│   ├── 3d/                      # Generated 3D models
│   └── code/                    # Generated code artifacts
└── training_data/
    ├── comparisons/             # A/B test results
    └── sessions/                # GRPO training data

docs.title: Why Filesystem
docs.text:
• User projects are files — code, configs, scripts. A filesystem is the natural storage medium
  • OpenHands and ComfyUI already operate on filesystem paths
  • Docker volumes provide persistence across container restarts
  • Easy to backup (tar/rsync) and inspect (ls/cat)

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Natural fit: Code and config files live on the filesystem
  • Tool compatibility: All tools (ComfyUI, OpenHands, Git) work natively with files
  • Simple backups: tar or rsync the volume
  • Inspectable: SSH in and cat any file for debugging
  • No ORM: No need to serialize/deserialize from a database

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• No built-in versioning: Must layer Git or manual backups for history
  • Concurrent access: No ACID transactions on file writes
  • No search: Filesystem search is slower than database queries (mitigated by MemPalace for semantic search)
  • Volume management: Docker volumes must be backed up separately from database dumps

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Architecture: Memory System
  • Admin: Backup and Restore

docs.title: ADR-004: Inference-Time MarsRL Verification
docs.text: Status: Accepted Date: 2026-01

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
LLM outputs can contain errors: syntax bugs in code, hallucinated facts, unsafe content, or incoherent responses. Two approaches exist for quality improvement:

  1. Training-time: Fine-tune the model to make fewer errors (RLHF, DPO, GRPO)
  2. Inference-time: Verify and fix outputs before returning them

Agent Swarm already uses GRPO training for model improvement, but training cycles are slow (hours/days) and models can still produce bad outputs.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Implement MarsRL — an inference-time Solver → Verifier → Corrector loop that catches and fixes errors before they reach the user.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
User Request
    → Solver (generate response)
    → Verifier (3-layer validation)
        Layer 1: AST parse (code only)
        Layer 2: Coherence (all responses)
        Layer 3: Safety (llama-guard-3)
    → If score < 0.60: Corrector (rewrite) → back to Verifier
    → If score ≥ 0.60: Return to user
    → After 2 iterations: Return best attempt

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Complementary to training: Training reduces error rate; MarsRL catches remaining errors
  • Immediate effect: No retraining needed — fixes work on the current model
  • Observable: Every verification step is traced in Langfuse with process-reward scores
  • Tunable: Thresholds, max iterations, and verifier layers are configurable
  • Named for: Mars — Roman god of war (aggressive quality defense) + RL (Reinforcement Learning, as it uses reward signals)

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Higher quality outputs: Catches syntax errors, repetition, and unsafe content
  • Immediate deployment: No model retraining required
  • Full observability: Every verification is traced with scores
  • Training signal: Failed verifications generate comparison pairs for GRPO training
  • Configurable strictness: Adjust thresholds per intent

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• Latency cost: Up to 3× token generation for failed responses (solver + 2 corrections)
  • Token cost: Corrector uses the same model, consuming additional inference tokens
  • False negatives: Simple heuristics (AST, coherence) may miss subtle errors
  • Complexity: Three-agent pipeline is harder to debug than direct inference

docs.title: Performance Impact
docs.text:
Scenario Latency Overhead Pass on first try (majority) ~200ms (verifier only) One correction round ~2–3s additional Max iterations (2) ~4–6s additional

In practice, ~80% of responses pass on the first try.

docs.title: Adding Agents
docs.text: How to create a new specialized agent in Agent Swarm.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
To add a new agent, you need:

  1. An agent class with a run() method
  2. A new intent in the Semantic Router (or map to an existing one)
  3. Router dispatch wiring
  4. A JWT-ACE capability profile

docs.title: Step 1: Create the Agent
docs.text:
Create a new file in agents/specialized/:

# agents/specialized/my_agent.py
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


@dataclass
class MyAgentResult:
    response: str
    metadata: dict


class MyAgent:
    """Handles MY_INTENT requests."""

    def __init__(self, config):
        self.config = config
        self.ollama_url = config.get("ollama_url", "http://localhost:11434")

    async def run(
        self,
        messages: list[dict],
        session_id: str,
        token: Optional[str] = None,
    ) -> MyAgentResult:
        """Process the user request."""
        user_message = messages[-1]["content"]
        logger.info(f"MyAgent processing: {user_message[:50]}...")

        # Your agent logic here
        result = await self._process(user_message)

        return MyAgentResult(
            response=result,
            metadata={"agent": "my_agent", "session_id": session_id},
        )

    async def _process(self, message: str) -> str:
        # Implementation
        return f"Processed: {message}"

docs.title: Step 2: Add Intent (Optional)
docs.text:
If your agent needs a new intent, update the Semantic Router:

# In agents/semantic_router.py, add to the intent list:
INTENTS = [
    # ... existing intents ...
    "MY_INTENT",
]

Update the router prompt to include the new intent description.

docs.title: Step 3: Wire Dispatch
docs.text:
In agents/router.py, add the dispatch case:

# In the route_request() method:
elif intent == "MY_INTENT":
    from agents.specialized.my_agent import MyAgent
    agent = MyAgent(self.config)
    result = await agent.run(messages, session_id, token)
    return result.response

docs.title: Step 4: Define Capabilities
docs.text:
Add the JWT-ACE capability profile in agents/intent_capabilities.py:

INTENT_CAPABILITIES = {
    # ... existing intents ...
    "MY_INTENT": {
        "tools": ["search", "custom_tool"],
        "level": "L3",
    },
}

docs.title: Step 5: Add Tests
docs.text:
# tests/test_my_agent.py
import pytest
from agents.specialized.my_agent import MyAgent


@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_my_agent_basic():
    config = {"ollama_url": "http://localhost:11434"}
    agent = MyAgent(config)
    result = await agent.run(
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "test input"}],
        session_id="test-session",
    )
    assert result.response
    assert result.metadata["agent"] == "my_agent"

docs.title: Using MarsRL Verification
docs.text:
If your agent should use MarsRL quality verification:

from agents.mars_loop import MarsRLLoop

class MyAgent:
    async def run(self, messages, session_id, token):
        mars = MarsRLLoop(config=self.config)
        result = await mars.run(
            messages=messages,
            intent="MY_INTENT",
            session_id=session_id,
            token=token,
        )
        return result

docs.title: Agent Design Guidelines
docs.text:
• Keep agents focused on one intent or capability
  • Use async/await for all I/O operations
  • Log with contextual information: logger.info(f"... session={session_id}")
  • Return structured results (dataclass), not raw strings
  • Handle errors gracefully — return error info in the result, don't crash

docs.title: Adding Tools
docs.text: How to create new tools that agents can use during execution.

docs.title: Tool Structure
docs.text:
Tools are Python functions registered in the tool registry. Each tool has:

  • A name (used in JWT-ACE capability tokens)
  • A description (shown to the LLM for tool selection)
  • An implementation function

docs.title: Create a Tool
docs.text:
# agents/tools/my_tool.py
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def my_tool(param1: str, param2: int = 10) -> dict:
    """
    Description shown to the LLM when selecting tools.

    Args:
        param1: What this parameter does
        param2: Optional parameter with default

    Returns:
        Result dictionary with output data
    """
    logger.info(f"my_tool called: param1={param1}, param2={param2}")

    # Tool implementation
    result = do_something(param1, param2)

    return {
        "status": "success",
        "output": result,
    }

docs.title: Register the Tool
docs.text:
Add the tool to the tool registry in agents/tools/__init__.py:

from agents.tools.my_tool import my_tool

TOOL_REGISTRY = {
    # ... existing tools ...
    "my_tool": {
        "function": my_tool,
        "description": "Does something useful with the given parameters",
        "parameters": {
            "param1": {"type": "string", "required": True},
            "param2": {"type": "integer", "required": False, "default": 10},
        },
    },
}

docs.title: Add to Capability Profiles
docs.text:
Update agents/intent_capabilities.py to allow specific intents to use the tool:

INTENT_CAPABILITIES = {
    "CODE": {
        "tools": ["file_ops", "terminal", "ast_tool", "my_tool"],
        "level": "L4",
    },
}

docs.title: Existing Tools
docs.text: Tool File Purpose file_ops tools/file_ops.py Read, write, list files terminal tools/terminal.py Execute shell commands ast_tool tools/ast_tool.py Python AST analysis search tools/search.py Web and document search ha_call_service tools/ha_tools.py Home Assistant service calls ha_turn_on tools/ha_tools.py Home Assistant device on ha_turn_off tools/ha_tools.py Home Assistant device off mqtt_publish tools/mqtt_tools.py MQTT message publishing

docs.title: Security Considerations
docs.text:
• Tools are gated by JWT-ACE tokens — an agent can only call tools listed in its token
  • Dangerous operations (file deletion, shell commands) should validate inputs
  • Never execute user input directly — sanitize and validate
  • Log all tool invocations with contextual info for audit trails

docs.title: Testing Tools
docs.text:
# tests/test_my_tool.py
from agents.tools.my_tool import my_tool


def test_my_tool_basic():
    result = my_tool(param1="test", param2=5)
    assert result["status"] == "success"
    assert "output" in result


def test_my_tool_default_param():
    result = my_tool(param1="test")
    assert result["status"] == "success"

docs.title: ComfyUI Workflows
docs.text: How to create and deploy custom image generation workflows.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Agent Swarm uses ComfyUI for all image generation. Workflows are JSON files that define the node graph for generation pipelines.

docs.title: Workflow Location
docs.text:
workflow_hunyuan_paint.json     # HunyuanDiT painting workflow
workflow_hunyuan_paint-2.json   # HunyuanDiT variant

Custom workflows go in the same directory or in agents/specialized/workflows/.

docs.title: Workflow Structure
docs.text:
ComfyUI workflows are JSON documents describing a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of processing nodes:

{
    "1": {
        "class_type": "CheckpointLoaderSimple",
        "inputs": {
            "ckpt_name": "flux1-schnell.safetensors"
        }
    },
    "2": {
        "class_type": "CLIPTextEncode",
        "inputs": {
            "text": "{{PROMPT}}",
            "clip": ["1", 1]
        }
    },
    "3": {
        "class_type": "KSampler",
        "inputs": {
            "model": ["1", 0],
            "positive": ["2", 0],
            "seed": "{{SEED}}",
            "steps": 20,
            "cfg": 7.0
        }
    }
}

docs.title: 1. Design in ComfyUI UI
docs.text:
1. Access ComfyUI at http://192.168.2.101:8188
  2. Build your node graph visually
  3. Click Save (API Format) to export the JSON

docs.title: 2. Add Template Variables
docs.text:
Replace dynamic values with template variables:

Variable Replaced With {{PROMPT}} User's text prompt {{NEGATIVE}} Negative prompt {{SEED}} Random or specified seed {{WIDTH}} Image width {{HEIGHT}} Image height {{STEPS}} Inference steps

docs.title: 3. Register the Workflow
docs.text:
Add the workflow to the image generation agent:

# In agents/specialized/image_gen.py
WORKFLOWS = {
    "flux-schnell": "workflows/flux_schnell.json",
    "sdxl": "workflows/sdxl.json",
    "hunyuan-paint": "workflow_hunyuan_paint.json",
    "custom": "workflows/my_custom.json",
}

docs.title: 4. Test
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/chat/completions \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Generate an image of a mountain sunset using custom workflow"}],
        "stream": false
    }'

docs.title: Available Pipelines
docs.text: Pipeline Model Best For VRAM FLUX.1-schnell FLUX Fast generation, good quality ~10 GB SDXL Stable Diffusion XL High resolution, detailed ~8 GB HunyuanDiT Hunyuan Painting style, Chinese art ~12 GB

└── images/
    ├── img_20260410_143022_abc123.png
    └── img_20260410_143022_abc123.json  # metadata

docs.text: Set up Agent Swarm for local development and testing.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.11+
  • Docker Desktop with GPU support
  • Git
  • NVIDIA drivers (for GPU inference)

docs.title: Clone and Setup
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/your-org/Agent_Swarm.git
cd Agent_Swarm

# Create virtual environment
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate  # Linux/Mac
# or: .venv\Scripts\activate  # Windows

# Install dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Start Ollama
docs.text:
# Pull required models
docker run -d --gpus all -p 11434:11434 \
    --name ollama -v ollama_data:/root/.ollama \
    ollama/ollama

docker exec ollama ollama pull qwen3.5:9b
docker exec ollama ollama pull nemotron-orchestrator:8b
docker exec ollama ollama pull llama-guard-3:8b

docs.title: Configure Environment
docs.text:
cp network.env.example network.env

Edit network.env for local development:

LOVELACE_IP=127.0.0.1
HOPPER_IP=127.0.0.1
TURING_IP=127.0.0.1
OLLAMA_HOST=http://localhost:11434
AGENT_RUNTIME_PORT=8008

docs.title: Run Agent Runtime
docs.text:
cd agents
python main.py

The runtime starts at http://localhost:8008.

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:

docs.title: Development Workflow
docs.text:
1. Create a feature branch: git checkout -b feature/my-change
  2. Make changes to agent code in agents/
  3. Test locally with pytest and manual curl requests
  4. Check logs in logs/ directory
  5. Submit a pull request

docs.title: Chat Completions API
docs.text: The primary API for interacting with Agent Swarm. OpenAI-compatible format.

docs.title: Endpoint
docs.text:
POST /v1/chat/completions

Via Gateway: POST http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/chat/completions

docs.title: Headers
docs.text: Header Required Value Content-Type Yes application/json

docs.title: Body
docs.text:
{
    "messages": [
        {"role": "system", "content": "Optional system prompt"},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Your message here"}
    ],
    "stream": true,
    "model": "qwen3.5:9b",
    "session_id": "optional-session-id",
    "owner_id": "optional-user-id",
    "dev_mode": false,
    "temperature": 0.7,
    "max_tokens": 4096
}

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description messages array required Chat messages (role + content) stream bool true Enable SSE streaming model string qwen3.5:9b Model override session_id string auto-generated Conversation session ID owner_id string "default" User identifier dev_mode bool false Enable developer tools temperature float 0.7 Sampling temperature max_tokens int 4096 Maximum response tokens

docs.title: Response (Non-Streaming)
docs.text:
{
    "id": "chatcmpl-abc123",
    "object": "chat.completion",
    "created": 1712764800,
    "model": "qwen3.5:9b",
    "choices": [
        {
            "index": 0,
            "message": {
                "role": "assistant",
                "content": "Here's your response..."
            },
            "finish_reason": "stop"
        }
    ],
    "usage": {
        "prompt_tokens": 50,
        "completion_tokens": 200,
        "total_tokens": 250
    },
    "metadata": {
        "intent": "CODE",
        "confidence": 0.92,
        "mars_score": 0.85,
        "trace_id": "trace-xyz789"
    }
}

docs.title: Response (Streaming)
docs.text:
With "stream": true, the response is Server-Sent Events:

data: {"event": "status", "data": "Classifying intent..."}

data: {"event": "status", "data": "Intent: CODE (0.92)"}

data: {"event": "thought", "data": "Analyzing the request..."}

data: {"event": "response", "data": "Here's the code:\n```python\n..."}

data: {"event": "response", "data": "```\n"}

data: [DONE]

docs.title: Event Types
docs.text: Event Description status Progress updates (intent classification, verification) thought Agent reasoning (visible in debug mode) response Content tokens tool_call Tool invocation details error Error information

docs.title: cURL
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/chat/completions \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Write a Python fibonacci function"}],
        "stream": false
    }'

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
import requests

response = requests.post(
    "http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/chat/completions",
    json={
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Write a Python fibonacci function"}],
        "stream": False,
    },
)
print(response.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"])

docs.title: Python (Streaming)
docs.text:
import requests

with requests.post(
    "http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/chat/completions",
    json={
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Explain Docker"}],
        "stream": True,
    },
    stream=True,
) as resp:
    for line in resp.iter_lines():
        if line:
            print(line.decode())

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
Browse by category or use the search bar to find answers.

  • General — What is Agent Swarm? Who is it for?
  • Models — Which models? How to switch? Performance?
  • Deployment — Hardware, Docker, networking
  • Security — Authentication, SPIRE, secrets
  • Troubleshooting FAQ — Quick fixes for common issues

docs.title: Can I run everything on a single machine?
docs.text: Yes. Merge all Docker Compose files and run all services on one machine. You'll need at least 32 GB RAM and an NVIDIA GPU.

docs.title: Do I need Docker?
docs.text: Yes. All services run as Docker containers managed by Docker Compose.

docs.title: What Linux distribution should I use?
docs.text: Ubuntu 22.04+ or Debian 12+ recommended. Any distribution with Docker Engine and NVIDIA Container Toolkit support will work.

docs.title: Can I run it on Windows or macOS?
docs.text: The Execution Node requires NVIDIA GPU passthrough, which works best on Linux. The Control and Gateway nodes can theoretically run on any Docker-capable OS, but Linux is recommended for all nodes.

docs.title: How do I update?
docs.text:
git pull origin main
docker compose build
docker compose up -d

See Updates.

docs.title: What ports need to be open?
docs.text: Only between your internal nodes. See Port Map for the full list. No ports need to be exposed to the internet.

docs.title: Can I use Kubernetes instead of Docker Compose?
docs.text: Not currently. The deployment is designed around Docker Compose. A Kubernetes migration is possible but not officially supported.

docs.title: How do I back up everything?
docs.text:
See Backup & Restore. Key items:

  • PostgreSQL database dump
  • workspace/ directory
  • network.env configuration
  • SPIRE configuration files

docs.title: What happens if a node goes down?
docs.text:
• Control Node down: Langfuse tracing and PostgreSQL are unavailable, but agents can still respond (with degraded observability)
  • Execution Node down: All inference stops — this is the critical node
  • Gateway Node down: External access is lost, but services still run internally

docs.title: What is Agent Swarm?
docs.text: Agent Swarm is a self-hosted, multi-agent AI platform that runs on your own hardware. It orchestrates multiple specialized AI agents — for chat, image generation, 3D modeling, voice, IoT control, and more — through a unified interface.

docs.title: Who is it for?
docs.text:
Anyone who wants a private, extensible AI assistant. The three main audiences are:

  • Users — interact via the Hive UI
  • Admins — deploy and manage the infrastructure
  • Developers — extend with custom agents and tools

docs.title: What hardware do I need?
docs.text:
At minimum:

  • One machine with an NVIDIA GPU (8 GB+ VRAM)
  • 32 GB RAM
  • 100 GB storage

The recommended setup uses three nodes (Control, Execution, Gateway). See Prerequisites.

docs.title: Is it free?
docs.text: Yes. Agent Swarm is open source. The LLMs run locally via Ollama — no API keys or subscriptions required.

docs.title: What models does it use?
docs.text:
By default:

Role Model Solver qwen3.5:9b Router nemotron-orchestrator:8b Verifier llama-guard-3:8b

You can switch to any Ollama-compatible model. See Switch Models.

docs.title: Can I use it without internet?
docs.text: Yes, once models are downloaded. All inference, storage, and processing happen on your local network. Internet is only needed for web searches in Research Mode.

docs.title: How is it different from ChatGPT/Claude?
docs.text:
• Private — your data never leaves your network
  • Multi-agent — specialized agents for different tasks
  • Self-hosted — runs on your hardware
  • Extensible — add custom agents, tools, and workflows
  • IoT-connected — controls smart home devices

docs.title: Which models are supported?
docs.text:
Any model available through Ollama. Popular choices:

  • Qwen series (2.5, 3.5) — strong general performance
  • Llama series (3.1, 3.3, Guard) — Meta's open models
  • Gemma series — Google's efficient models
  • Mistral/Mixtral — fast inference
  • DeepSeek — coding-focused
  • Nemotron — NVIDIA's optimized models

docs.title: How do I add a new model?
docs.text:
docker exec ollama ollama pull <model-name>

See Add a New Model.

docs.title: Can I use different models for different tasks?
docs.text:
Yes. The system uses three model roles:

  • Solver — handles the main response generation
  • Router — classifies user intent
  • Verifier — checks response quality and safety

Each can be a different model. Configure in network.env.

docs.title: How much VRAM do I need?
docs.text:
Model Size VRAM (q4_K_M) VRAM (FP16) 3B ~2 GB ~6 GB 7–9B ~5 GB ~18 GB 14B ~9 GB ~28 GB 32B ~20 GB ~64 GB

Agent Swarm loads up to 3 models by default. Plan VRAM accordingly.

docs.title: Can I use API-based models (OpenAI, Anthropic)?
docs.text: Not out of the box. The system is designed for local Ollama models. However, because it uses the OpenAI-compatible API format, you could in theory point the solver at an external endpoint by changing the API URL configuration.

docs.title: How do I check which models are loaded?
docs.text: curl http://192.168.2.101:11434/api/tags | python -m json.tool

docs.title: Why is inference slow?
docs.text:
See Performance Tuning. Common causes:

  • VRAM pressure (too many models loaded)
  • Large context windows
  • Flash Attention disabled
  • Thermal throttling

docs.title: Is my data private?
docs.text: Yes. All data stays on your local network. No telemetry or data is sent externally. Models run locally via Ollama.

docs.title: How is authentication handled?
docs.text: Agent Swarm uses JWT profiles managed through SPIRE for service-to-service authentication. The Hive UI currently operates on the local network without user authentication (it's designed for trusted, private networks).

docs.title: What is SPIRE?
docs.text:
SPIRE (SPIFFE Runtime Environment) provides cryptographic identity to each service. It issues short-lived X.509 certificates (SVIDs) that services use to authenticate with each other over mTLS.

See Security Model.

docs.title: How are secrets managed?
docs.text:
Secrets are stored in network.env and injected as environment variables. For production hardening:

  • Use Docker secrets instead of environment variables
  • Rotate credentials regularly
  • See Secrets Management

docs.title: Can I expose it to the internet?
docs.text:
This is not recommended without additional security measures:

  • Add proper user authentication (OAuth2/OIDC)
  • Enable TLS on all endpoints
  • Set up firewall rules
  • Implement rate limiting
  • Regular security updates

docs.title: Is the Verifier a safety filter?
docs.text: Yes. The MarsRL verifier (llama-guard-3:8b) checks every response for safety, quality, and policy compliance before sending it to the user. Responses that fail verification are regenerated.

docs.title: What about prompt injection?
docs.text:
The system uses several defenses:

  • System prompts are protected from overwrite
  • The Verifier checks for suspicious outputs
  • Tool execution is sandboxed
  • File operations are scoped to the workspace directory

docs.title: Troubleshooting FAQ
docs.text: Quick answers to common issues. For detailed guides, see the Troubleshooting section.

docs.title: The chat doesn't respond
docs.text:
1. Check if the Agent Runtime is running: docker ps | grep agent-runtime
  2. Check if Ollama is healthy: curl http://192.168.2.101:11434/api/tags
  3. Check logs: docker logs agent-runtime --tail 50

docs.title: Image generation fails
docs.text:
1. Verify ComfyUI is running: curl http://192.168.2.101:8188/system_stats
  2. Check GPU memory: docker exec ollama nvidia-smi
  3. Ollama may need to unload models to free VRAM

docs.title: Voice doesn't work
docs.text:
1. Check microphone permissions in the browser
  2. Verify Whisper: curl http://192.168.2.101:9000/health
  3. Verify Piper: curl http://192.168.2.101:5500/health

docs.title: IoT commands don't work
docs.text:
1. Verify Home Assistant connection: check HA_URL and HA_TOKEN in network.env
  2. Test the API directly: curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $HA_TOKEN" http://<ha-ip>:8123/api/

docs.title: "Model not found" errors
docs.text:
The requested model isn't pulled yet:

docker exec ollama ollama pull <model-name>

docs.title: Everything is slow
docs.text:
See Performance Tuning. Quick checks:

  • GPU VRAM usage: nvidia-smi
  • Too many models loaded: reduce OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS
  • Network latency: ping 192.168.2.101

docs.title: How do I check if all services are running?
docs.text:
docker compose ps                # On each node
curl http://192.168.2.103/health   # Gateway health check

docs.title: Where are the logs?
docs.text:
• Docker logs: docker logs <container-name> --tail 100
  • Grafana/Loki: http://192.168.2.103:3001
  • Langfuse traces: http://192.168.2.102:3000
  • Local log files: logs/ directory

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Welcome to Agent Swarm. This section helps you get oriented regardless of your role.

docs.title: Choose Your Starting Point
docs.text: Guide Audience What You'll Learn Overview Everyone What Agent Swarm is, what it can do, and how it's built Quickstart: Users End users Access the Hive UI, start chatting, explore features Quickstart: Admins System admins Deploy the 3-node cluster from scratch Quickstart: Developers Contributors Clone the repo, run locally, make your first change Core Concepts Everyone Key terms and mental models for understanding the system

docs.title: System at a Glance
docs.text: Property Value Version 3.4 Status Phase 6 Complete Nodes 3 (Control, Execution, Gateway) Primary Model qwen3.5:9b Security SPIFFE/SPIRE + JWT-ACE (MAESTRO L1–L7) External Dependencies None — fully self-hosted

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text: Key mental models for understanding Agent Swarm.

docs.title: Agents
docs.text:
An agent is a specialized AI worker with a defined role, model, and set of tools. The system has several types:

Agent Model Purpose Solver qwen3.5:9b Generates initial responses to user requests Router nemotron-orchestrator:8b Classifies user intent into one of 14 categories Verifier llama-guard-3:8b Validates output safety and correctness Corrector qwen3.5:9b Fixes problems identified by the Verifier Coordinator qwen3:14b Orchestrates multi-step tasks across workers

Specialized agents handle domain-specific work: image generation, 3D modeling, voice synthesis, IoT control, and more.

docs.title: Intents
docs.text:
Every user message is classified into an intent by the Semantic Router. The intent determines which agent handles the request and what tools are available.

Intent Description Example CONVERSATION General chat, factual questions "What's the capital of France?" CODE Software engineering, debugging "Write a Python sort function" DEVOPS Infrastructure, Docker, CI/CD "How do I set up nginx?" DATA SQL, analytics, CSV processing "Analyze this dataset" IMAGE 2D visual art, photos "Generate a sunset painting" 3D 3D models, meshes "Create a low-poly tree" ACTION_FIGURE Posable action figures "Design a robot action figure" RESEARCH Deep knowledge synthesis "Compare React vs Vue" DOCUMENTATION Writing, formatting, summaries "Rewrite this README" TRAIN Teaching the system new rules "Remember that I prefer dark mode" IOT_CONTROL Smart home device control "Turn on the living room lights" IOT_DEV Firmware, circuits, MQTT "Write ESP32 code for a sensor" VISION Analyzing existing images "What's in this screenshot?" COORDINATE Complex multi-step tasks "Build and deploy a web app"

docs.title: MarsRL Loop
docs.text:
MarsRL (Mars Reinforcement Learning) is the inference-time quality verification loop at the heart of Agent Swarm. Every coding request passes through it:

graph LR
    A[User Request] --> B[Solver]
    B -->|Initial Response| C[Verifier]
    C -->|Score ≥ 0.60| D[Pass ✓]
    C -->|Score < 0.60| E[Corrector]
    E -->|Fixed Response| C
    C -->|Max 2 iterations| D

docs.title: Verifier Layers
docs.text:
Layer What It Checks Score Penalty Hard Block? AST Parse Python syntax validity −0.40 No Coherence Non-empty, no repetition loops −0.25 No Safety Content safety (llama-guard-3) Score → 0.0 Yes

The pass threshold is 0.60. Every interaction is traced in Langfuse with its process-reward score.

docs.title: SPIFFE / SPIRE
docs.text:
SPIFFE (Secure Production Identity Framework for Everyone) provides cryptographic identity to every workload in the system. SPIRE is the runtime that implements SPIFFE.

  • Every Docker container gets an X.509 certificate (SVID) proving its identity
  • Trust domain: home-ai-lab
  • Services authenticate to each other using mutual TLS — no passwords or API keys
  • Example SPIFFE ID: spiffe://home-ai-lab/execution-node

docs.title: JWT-ACE Tokens
docs.text:
JWT-ACE (JSON Web Token — Authorization for Constrained Environments) are ephemeral, per-request capability tokens.

  • Generated when a request is routed to an agent
  • Scoped to the specific intent and tools needed
  • Short-lived — they expire after the request completes
  • Encode the security level required (L1–L7)
sequenceDiagram
    participant Router
    participant TokenIssuer
    participant Agent

    Router->>TokenIssuer: Issue token for CODE intent
    TokenIssuer-->>Router: JWT-ACE (tools: file_ops, terminal)
    Router->>Agent: Execute with token
    Agent->>Agent: Verify token scope before each tool call

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
A skill is a registered capability that agents can invoke. Skills are managed by the Skill Registry and exposed via the Model Context Protocol (MCP).

Each skill has:

  • Triggers: Intents, keywords, or regex patterns that activate it
  • Required capabilities: What JWT-ACE permissions it needs
  • Security level: Minimum MAESTRO level required
  • Handler: The function that executes the skill

Examples: read_file, web_search, run_command, get_device_state, create_simulation.

docs.title: Governance
docs.text:
The governance system gates sensitive operations behind an approval workflow. When an agent needs to install a package, use a new model, or escalate permissions, it submits a governance request.

stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PENDING: Request submitted
    PENDING --> ASSESSING: Security + architect review
    ASSESSING --> APPROVED: Safe + compatible
    ASSESSING --> REJECTED: Unsafe or incompatible
    APPROVED --> COMPLETED: Action executed
    APPROVED --> FAILED: Execution error

docs.title: Three-Tier Topology
docs.text:
Tier Node Purpose Control Plane Dell Hopper (192.168.2.102) Identity, databases, observability, memory Execution Plane Lovelace (192.168.2.101) GPU inference, agent runtime, creative tools Gateway Dell Turing (192.168.2.103) Reverse proxy, monitoring, secondary inference

The tiers are connected over a flat LAN (192.168.2.0/24). Traefik on the Gateway routes external traffic to the appropriate backend.

  • Security Model — SPIFFE, JWT-ACE, MAESTRO in depth

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Agent Swarm is a self-hosted, distributed multi-agent AI system that runs entirely on local hardware. No data leaves your network. No external AI APIs are called. Everything — inference, storage, identity, monitoring — runs on three physical machines connected over a local LAN.

docs.title: Intelligent Chat and Coding
docs.text: Every request goes through a MarsRL quality loop: a Solver generates a response, a Verifier validates it (syntax, coherence, safety), and a Corrector fixes problems. The result is higher-quality output than a single model pass.

docs.title: Creative Media
docs.text:
• Image generation using ComfyUI with FLUX and Stable Diffusion XL pipelines
  • 3D model generation using TripoSG and Hunyuan3D
  • Action figure design with articulated joint specifications for 3D printing

docs.title: Voice Interaction
docs.text: BMO — a physical robot character — responds with a cloned voice via RVC synthesis. Text-to-speech is handled by Qwen3-TTS (1.7B parameters) running locally.

docs.title: Smart Home Control
docs.text: Natural language control of Home Assistant devices. Ask the system to turn on lights, check sensor readings, or manage automations. Hardware prototyping is supported through Wokwi ESP32/Arduino simulation.

docs.title: Autonomous Code Execution
docs.text: Sandboxed VS Code environments (OpenHands) for executing code in isolation. The system can write, test, and iterate on code without affecting the host.

docs.title: Architecture at a Glance
docs.text:
The system runs across three nodes:

Node Machine IP Role Control Plane Dell Hopper 192.168.2.102 Identity (SPIRE), databases (PostgreSQL, ClickHouse), observability (Langfuse), memory (MemPalace) Execution Plane Lovelace (RTX 5060 Ti 16GB) 192.168.2.101 GPU inference (Ollama), Agent Runtime (FastAPI), ComfyUI, Voice Engine, OpenHands Gateway Dell PowerEdge Turing (RTX 3070 Ti 8GB) 192.168.2.103 Reverse proxy (Traefik), monitoring (Prometheus, Grafana, Loki), secondary inference 
graph LR
    User([User]) --> Gateway
    Gateway[Gateway · Turing] -->|/swarm/*| Execution[Execution · Lovelace]
    Execution -->|identity| Control[Control · Hopper]
    Execution -->|traces| Control
    Execution -->|memory| Control
    Gateway -->|metrics| Gateway

docs.title: Security Model
docs.text:
• SPIFFE/SPIRE: Every service gets a cryptographic workload identity (X.509 SVID)
  • JWT-ACE: Each request gets an ephemeral capability token scoped to its intent
  • MAESTRO: L1–L7 security framework (98% compliant)
  • Output Validation: 3-layer verification — AST parse, coherence heuristics, llama-guard-3 safety check

docs.title: Step 1: Clone the Repository
docs.text:
On your development machine:

git clone https://github.com/Misterobots/Agent_Swarm.git
cd Agent_Swarm

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Network
docs.text:
Edit network.env at the repo root with your actual IP addresses:

LOVELACE_IP=192.168.2.101
HOPPER_IP=192.168.2.102
TURING_IP=192.168.2.103
HOME_ASSISTANT_IP=192.168.2.100

Credentials

Update all passwords in network.env before deploying. Never use default passwords in production.

docs.title: Step 3: Deploy Control Plane
docs.text:
SSH to the Control Node and start the services:

ssh ControlPlane
cd ~/Agent_Swarm/control_plane
docker compose up -d

Verify SPIRE server is running:

docker exec spire-server /opt/spire/bin/spire-server healthcheck

docs.title: Step 4: Deploy Execution Plane
docs.text:
On the Execution Node:

cd execution_plane
docker compose up -d

This starts Ollama, Agent Runtime, ComfyUI, Voice Engine, and OpenHands. The first startup will pull LLM models (several GB).

Verify the Agent Runtime is online:

curl http://localhost:8008/
# Expected: {"status": "online", "system": "Home AI Lab Swarm"}

docs.title: Step 5: Deploy Gateway
docs.text:
SSH to the Gateway Node:

ssh Turing
cd ~/Home_AI_Lab/turing_gateway
docker compose up -d

This starts Traefik, Prometheus, Grafana, Loki, and the monitoring stack.

docs.title: Step 6: Verify Deployment
docs.text:
Run smoke tests from any node:

# Check Agent Runtime
curl http://192.168.2.101:8008/v1/models

# Check Ollama inference
curl http://192.168.2.101:11434/api/tags

# Check Grafana
curl -s http://192.168.2.103:3001/api/health

# Check Langfuse
curl -s http://192.168.2.102:3000/api/public/health

All Clear

If all four return valid JSON, the cluster is operational. Open http://192.168.2.103 to access the Hive UI.

docs.title: Quickstart: Developers
docs.text: Get the Agent Swarm codebase running locally for development.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.11+
  • Docker Desktop (for dependent services)
  • Git
  • A running Ollama instance (local or remote)

docs.title: Step 1: Clone and Install
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/Misterobots/Agent_Swarm.git
cd Agent_Swarm
pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Step 2: Configure for Local Development
docs.text:
Create a local override in agents/config.py or set environment variables:

export OLLAMA_HOST=http://localhost:11434
export LLM_PROVIDER=ollama
export LANGFUSE_HOST=http://localhost:3000  # or disable tracing

Minimal Setup

You only need Ollama running locally to work on the agent runtime. The control plane services (SPIRE, Langfuse, PostgreSQL) are optional for development — the runtime falls back gracefully when they're unavailable.

docs.title: Step 3: Pull Required Models
docs.text:
ollama pull qwen3.5:9b                  # Solver / Corrector
ollama pull nemotron-orchestrator:8b     # Router
ollama pull llama-guard-3:8b             # Safety verifier
ollama pull llama3.2:3b                  # Lightweight tasks

docs.title: Step 4: Start the Agent Runtime
docs.text:
cd Agent_Swarm
uvicorn agents.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --log-level debug --reload

The --reload flag enables hot-reloading on code changes.

Verify it's running:

curl http://localhost:8000/
# {"status": "online", "system": "Home AI Lab Swarm"}

curl http://localhost:8000/v1/models
# Returns available model list

docs.title: Step 5: Send a Test Request
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Write a Python hello world"}],
    "model": "default",
    "stream": false
  }'

docs.title: Step 6: Run Tests
docs.text: pytest tests/ -v

docs.title: What Happens Behind the Scenes
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant You
    participant UI as Hive UI
    participant GW as Gateway (Traefik)
    participant RT as Agent Runtime
    participant LLM as Ollama (Solver)
    participant V as Verifier

    You->>UI: Type message
    UI->>GW: POST /swarm/v1/chat/completions

docs.title: Coordinator
docs.text: Multi-agent orchestration for complex, multi-step tasks.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: File Purpose agents/coordinator.py Coordinator class — phases and workers

docs.title: When Used
docs.text:
The Coordinator handles COORDINATE and RESEARCH intents — tasks that require:

  • Breaking a problem into subtasks
  • Parallel research or investigation
  • Multiple agent capabilities in one request
  • Verification of the assembled result

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Decompose] --> B[Research]
    B --> C[Synthesize]
    C --> D[Implement]
    D --> E[Verify]
Phase Description Workers Decompose LLM breaks the task into subtasks 1 (coordinator) Research Workers investigate in parallel Up to 5 Synthesize Merge findings into a plan 1 (coordinator) Implement Execute the plan 1–3 Verify Fresh worker validates results 1

docs.title: Worker Roles
docs.text:
WORKERS = {
    "architect": ArchitectAgent,
    "coder": ArchitectAgent,
    "devops": ArchitectAgent,
    "analyst": DataAnalyst,
    "researcher": ResearchWorker,
    "verifier": VerificationWorker,
}

docs.title: Scratchpad
docs.text:
Workers communicate via a shared scratchpad — a filesystem directory containing intermediate artifacts:

workspace/coordinator_sessions/{session_id}/
├── plan.json           # Decomposed task plan
├── research_1.md       # Worker 1 findings
├── research_2.md       # Worker 2 findings
├── synthesis.md        # Merged findings
├── implementation.md   # Final output
└── verification.md     # Verification report

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Setting Value Description Max workers 5 Parallel research workers Timeout per phase 120s Phase execution limit Max total time 600s Total coordination limit

docs.title: Example Flow
docs.text:
User: "Compare Python web frameworks for a REST API and build a prototype"

  1. Decompose: Split into "research frameworks" + "build prototype"
  2. Research: 3 workers investigate Flask, FastAPI, Django REST
  3. Synthesize: Merge into comparison table with recommendation
  4. Implement: Coder builds FastAPI prototype
  5. Verify: Verifier checks code runs and matches requirements

docs.title: Dispatcher
docs.text: Event bus and async task queue management.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: File Purpose agents/dispatcher.py Dispatcher class — queue routing and concurrency

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
The Dispatcher manages async task execution for long-running operations that shouldn't block the chat response:

  • Image generation (10–60 seconds)
  • 3D model rendering (30–120 seconds)
  • Batch operations

docs.title: Queue Architecture
docs.text:
graph LR
    API[API Request] --> D[Dispatcher]
    D --> Q1["queue:default · 5 workers"]
    D --> Q2["queue:image · 2 workers"]
    D --> Q3["queue:3d · 1 worker"]
    D --> Q4["queue:action_figure · 1 worker"]

docs.title: Queue Configuration
docs.text:
Queue Workers Purpose default 5 Chat, code, general tasks image 2 ComfyUI image generation 3d 1 3D mesh reconstruction action_figure 1 Articulated figure design

Worker counts are limited to prevent GPU memory exhaustion.

docs.title: Task Lifecycle
docs.text:
1. API receives request, router classifies intent
  2. If async (IMAGE, 3D): Dispatcher queues the task, returns task ID immediately
  3. Worker picks up task from queue
  4. Worker executes (calls ComfyUI, 3D pipeline, etc.)
  5. Result is stored; task status updated to completed
  6. Client polls GET /v1/tasks/{task_id} for result

docs.title: Redis Backend
docs.text:
The Dispatcher uses Redis for queue persistence:

  • Tasks survive container restarts
  • Workers dequeue atomically
  • Failed tasks can be retried

docs.title: Governance
docs.text: Request approval workflow for privileged operations.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: File Purpose agents/governance.py Governance engine — state machine, assessment, persistence

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: When an agent attempts a privileged or risky operation (dangerous commands, system changes), the Governance module intercepts and routes the request through an approval workflow.

docs.title: State Machine
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PendingReview: Submit request
    PendingReview --> Approved: Admin approves
    PendingReview --> Denied: Admin denies
    PendingReview --> AutoApproved: Policy auto-approve
    PendingReview --> Expired: Timeout (24h)
    Approved --> [*]: Execute action
    Denied --> [*]
    AutoApproved --> [*]: Execute action
    Expired --> [*]

docs.title: Request Types
docs.text: Type Triggers execute_command Blocklisted shell commands file_delete Deleting important files system_modify Network/system changes privilege_escalation Requesting higher security level

docs.title: Assessment
docs.text:
The governance engine performs a risk assessment:

risk = assess_risk(action, command, context)
# Returns: LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, CRITICAL
Risk Action LOW Auto-approve MEDIUM Notify admin, execute HIGH Require admin approval CRITICAL Block, notify admin

docs.title: Storage
docs.text: Governance state is persisted in workflow_state/governance_requests.json.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:

  • User Guide: Governance — user-facing guide

docs.title: GPU Allocator
docs.text: VRAM reservation and model scheduling.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: File Purpose agents/gpu_allocator.py GPU memory management

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
The GPU Allocator tracks VRAM usage and prevents out-of-memory errors by:

  • Tracking which models are loaded in VRAM
  • Preventing concurrent operations that exceed VRAM budget
  • Coordinating between Ollama, ComfyUI, and Voice Engine

docs.title: Execution Node (RTX 5060 Ti — 16 GB)
docs.text:
Consumer VRAM Priority qwen3.5:9b ~6 GB High nemotron-orchestrator:8b ~5 GB High llama-guard-3:8b ~5 GB Medium ComfyUI (FLUX) ~10 GB On-demand Voice (Qwen3-TTS) ~3 GB On-demand

Not All Fit Simultaneously

Total VRAM needed for all models exceeds 16 GB. The allocator and Ollama cooperate to load/unload models dynamically.

docs.title: Interaction with Ollama
docs.text:
Ollama handles its own model lifecycle:

Ollama Setting Effect OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS=3 Maximum 3 models in VRAM OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=10m Unload idle models after 10 min

The GPU Allocator complements Ollama by scheduling non-Ollama GPU tasks (ComfyUI, voice synthesis) to avoid conflicts.

docs.title: Pipelines
docs.text: Pipeline Model VRAM Speed Best For FLUX.1-schnell FLUX ~10 GB Fast (3–8s) General purpose SDXL Stable Diffusion XL ~8 GB Medium (10–20s) High detail HunyuanDiT Hunyuan ~12 GB Slow (20–40s) Painting style

docs.title: Processing Flow
docs.text:
1. User message classified as IMAGE intent
  2. Image Agent extracts generation parameters from user prompt
  3. Selects appropriate workflow/pipeline
  4. Submits workflow to ComfyUI API (http://192.168.2.101:8188)
  5. Polls for completion
  6. Saves output to delivered_artifacts/images/
  7. Returns image path and metadata

docs.text: Parameter Default Description steps 20 Inference steps cfg_scale 7.0 Classifier-free guidance width 1024 Image width height 1024 Image height seed random Reproducibility seed negative_prompt "" What to avoid

docs.title: Skills Memory Integration
docs.text:
Visual rules from Skills Memory are applied:

rules = memory.get_relevant_rules(prompt, "visual_rules")

docs.title: MarsRL Loop
docs.text: Inference-time quality verification: Solver → Verifier → Corrector.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: File Purpose agents/mars_loop.py MarsRLLoop class — orchestration agents/architect_agent.py Solver (response generation) agents/verifier_agent.py Verifier (3-layer validation) agents/corrector_agent.py Corrector (error rewriting)

docs.title: run() Method
docs.text:
async def run(
    messages: list[dict],
    intent: str,
    session_id: str,
    token: Optional[str] = None,
    stream_callback: Optional[Callable] = None,
) -> MarsLoopResult

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Solver generates response
2. Verifier runs 3 checks:
   a. AST parse (code only)
   b. Coherence check
   c. Safety check (llama-guard-3)
3. If score ≥ 0.60: PASS → return
4. If score < 0.60 and iterations < 2:
   a. Corrector rewrites response
   b. Go to step 2
5. If iterations ≥ 2: return best attempt

docs.title: MarsLoopResult
docs.text:
@dataclass
class MarsLoopResult:
    response: str
    iterations: int
    solver_score: float
    corrector_invoked: bool
    final_score: float
    trace_id: Optional[str]
    token: Optional[str]
    template_metadata: dict
    metadata: dict

docs.title: Layer 1: AST Parse
docs.text:
try:
    ast.parse(code_content)
    # Score unchanged
except SyntaxError:
    score -= 0.40

Only runs when the response contains Python code blocks.

docs.title: Layer 2: Coherence
docs.text:
Checks for:

  • Non-empty response
  • No repetition loops (same phrase repeated 3+ times)
  • Response addresses the user's question

Score penalty: −0.25 per failed check.

docs.title: Layer 3: Safety
docs.text: Uses llama-guard-3:8b to check for harmful content. A safety failure forces score to 0.0 and blocks the response.

docs.title: Langfuse Integration
docs.text:
Every run creates:

  • A trace with session ID and intent metadata
  • Spans for each step (solver, verifier, corrector)
  • Scores recorded as float 0.0–1.0

docs.title: Router
docs.text: The Semantic Router is the entry point for all user requests. It classifies intent, issues capability tokens, and dispatches to the appropriate agent.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: File Purpose agents/semantic_router.py Intent classification (Nemotron LLM) agents/router.py Request handling, token issuance, dispatch agents/intent_capabilities.py Intent → capability mapping

docs.title: Intent Classification
docs.text:
Uses nemotron-orchestrator:8b to classify messages into 14 intents:

INTENTS = [
    "CONVERSATION", "CODE", "DEVOPS", "DATA",
    "IMAGE", "3D", "ACTION_FIGURE",
    "RESEARCH", "DOCUMENTATION", "TRAIN",
    "IOT_CONTROL", "IOT_DEV", "VISION", "COORDINATE",
]

docs.title: Classification Prompt
docs.text:
The router sends a structured prompt to the LLM with:

  • The user's message
  • List of available intents with descriptions
  • Instructions to return JSON: {"intent": "...", "confidence": 0.0-1.0, "reasoning": "..."}

docs.title: Fallback Logic
docs.text: Condition Action Confidence ≥ 0.60 Accept intent Confidence < 0.60 Retry with stronger prompt Intent = AMBIGUOUS Retry or ask disambiguation question Timeout / error Fallback to CONVERSATION

docs.title: Token Issuance
docs.text:
After classification, the router generates a JWT-ACE token:

token = issue_token(
    intent="CODE",
    tools=["file_ops", "terminal", "ast_tool"],
    level="L4",
    session_id=session_id,
    owner_id=owner_id,
)

docs.title: Dispatch Table
docs.text: Intent Pipeline CODE, CONVERSATION, DEVOPS, DATA, DOCUMENTATION MarsRL Loop IMAGE Image Agent 3D, ACTION_FIGURE 3D Pipeline COORDINATE, RESEARCH Coordinator IOT_CONTROL IoT Agent IOT_DEV IoT Dev Agent TRAIN Memory System VISION Vision Agent

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Setting Value Description Router model nemotron-orchestrator:8b Intent classification LLM Min confidence 0.60 Below this triggers retry Max retries 2 Classification retry limit Timeout 10s LLM response timeout

docs.title: Langfuse
docs.text: LLM observability and tracing service.

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text: Property Value Node Control Plane (192.168.2.102) Port 3000 URL http://192.168.2.102:3000 Backend ClickHouse + PostgreSQL Compose control_plane/docker-compose.yml

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Langfuse captures end-to-end traces for every LLM interaction in Agent Swarm:

  • Traces: One per user request
  • Spans: Solver, Verifier, Corrector steps
  • Scores: Process-reward scores (0.0–1.0)
  • Generations: Individual LLM calls with tokens/latency
  • Metadata: Intent, model, template version

docs.title: Integration
docs.text:
The Agent Runtime uses the Langfuse Python SDK:

from langfuse import Langfuse

lf = Langfuse(
    public_key=os.environ["LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY"],
    secret_key=os.environ["LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY"],
    host=os.environ["LANGFUSE_HOST"],
)

trace = lf.trace(name="chat_request", session_id=session_id)
span = trace.span(name="solver")
# ... generation ...
span.end()
trace.score(name="verification", value=0.85)

docs.title: Procedures
docs.text:
Step-by-step operational runbooks for common administrative tasks.

Procedure Description Add a New Model Pull and configure a new Ollama model Switch Models Change the solver, router, or verifier model Rotate SPIRE Keys Regenerate SPIRE join tokens and certificates Configure Alerting Set up Prometheus alerts and AlertManager Onboard a User Set up a new user with access Disaster Recovery Full system recovery from backup GPU Troubleshooting Diagnose and fix GPU issues Database Maintenance PostgreSQL vacuum, backup, restore Log Analysis Query and analyze logs in Loki Performance Tuning Optimize inference speed and throughput

docs.title: Add a New Model
docs.text: Pull a new model from the Ollama registry and make it available.

docs.title: 1. Check Available VRAM
docs.text:
docker exec ollama nvidia-smi

Verify there's enough VRAM for the new model.

docs.title: 2. Pull the Model
docs.text:
docker exec ollama ollama pull <model-name>:<tag>

Example:

docker exec ollama ollama pull gemma3:12b

docs.title: 3. Test the Model
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://192.168.2.101:11434/v1/chat/completions \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
        "model": "gemma3:12b",
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hello, how are you?"}]
    }'

docs.title: 4. (Optional) Create a Modelfile
docs.text:
For custom system prompts or parameters:

cat > /tmp/Modelfile << 'EOF'
FROM gemma3:12b
PARAMETER temperature 0.7
SYSTEM "You are a helpful assistant."
EOF

docker cp /tmp/Modelfile ollama:/tmp/Modelfile
docker exec ollama ollama create gemma3-custom -f /tmp/Modelfile

docs.title: 5. Verify
docs.text:
curl http://192.168.2.101:11434/api/tags | python -m json.tool

The model should appear in the list.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Model downloads can be large (2–20 GB). Ensure sufficient disk space.
  • The Gateway's secondary Ollama (port 11435) can also host models.

docs.title: Configure Alerting
docs.text: Set up Prometheus alerts and AlertManager notifications.

docs.title: 1. Define Alert Rules
docs.text:
Edit turing_gateway/config/prometheus/alert_rules.yml:

groups:
  - name: agent-swarm
    rules:
      - alert: HighErrorRate
        expr: rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m]) > 0.05
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "High error rate detected"

      - alert: OllamaDown
        expr: up{job="ollama"} == 0
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Ollama is unreachable"

docs.title: 2. Configure AlertManager
docs.text:
Edit turing_gateway/config/alertmanager/alertmanager.yml:

global:
  smtp_smarthost: 'smtp.gmail.com:587'
  smtp_from: 'alerts@example.com'

route:
  receiver: 'default'
  routes:
    - match:
        severity: critical
      receiver: 'email'

receivers:
  - name: 'default'
    webhook_configs:
      - url: 'http://ntfy-server/alerts'
  - name: 'email'
    email_configs:
      - to: 'admin@example.com'

docs.title: 3. Restart Services
docs.text: docker compose restart prometheus alertmanager

docs.title: 4. Test Alerts
docs.text:
# Manually fire a test alert
curl -X POST http://192.168.2.103:9093/api/v1/alerts \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '[{"labels":{"alertname":"TestAlert","severity":"warning"},"annotations":{"summary":"Test alert"}}]'

Check that the notification arrives.

docs.title: Database Maintenance
docs.text: PostgreSQL maintenance tasks: vacuum, indexing, backup verification.

docs.title: Routine Vacuum
docs.text:
# Vacuum analyze all tables
docker compose exec postgres psql -U postgres -c "VACUUM ANALYZE;"

docs.title: Check Database Size
docs.text:
docker compose exec postgres psql -U postgres -c \
    "SELECT pg_size_pretty(pg_database_size('agent_swarm'));"

docs.title: Verify Backup Integrity
docs.text:
# Test restore to a temporary database
docker compose exec postgres createdb -U postgres test_restore
docker compose exec -T postgres pg_restore -U postgres -d test_restore < backup.dump
docker compose exec postgres dropdb -U postgres test_restore

docs.title: pgvector Maintenance
docs.text:
# Reindex vector indexes
docker compose exec postgres psql -U postgres -c \
    "REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_embeddings_vector;"

docs.title: Scheduled Maintenance
docs.text:
Add to crontab:

# Weekly vacuum
0 4 * * 0 docker compose -f /opt/Agent_Swarm/control_plane/docker-compose.yml exec -T postgres psql -U postgres -c "VACUUM ANALYZE;"

docs.title: Disaster Recovery
docs.text: Full system recovery from backup.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Access to backup files (see Backup & Restore)
  • Fresh node deployments ready

docs.title: 1. Deploy Fresh Nodes
docs.text:
Follow the deployment guides in order:

  1. Control Plane
  2. Execution Plane
  3. Gateway

docs.title: 2. Restore PostgreSQL
docs.text:
# On Control Node
docker compose exec -T postgres pg_restore \
    -U postgres -d postgres --clean \
    < /backups/postgres.dump

docs.title: 3. Restore Workspace
docs.text:
# On Execution Node
docker cp workspace.tar.gz agent-runtime:/tmp/
docker compose exec agent-runtime tar xzf /tmp/workspace.tar.gz -C /

docs.title: 4. Re-Pull Models
docs.text:
docker exec ollama ollama pull qwen3.5:9b
docker exec ollama ollama pull nemotron-orchestrator:8b
docker exec ollama ollama pull llama-guard-3:8b

docs.title: 5. Re-Attest SPIRE Agents
docs.text: Generate fresh join tokens and attest both agents. See Rotate SPIRE Keys.

docs.title: 6. Verify
docs.text: Run the full Post-Deployment Verification.

docs.title: Recovery Time Estimate
docs.text: Component Time Deploy nodes 30 min Restore databases 10 min Pull models 20 min SPIRE attestation 5 min Verification 10 min Total ~75 min

docs.title: GPU Troubleshooting
docs.text: Diagnose and resolve GPU-related issues.

docs.title: Quick Diagnostic
docs.text:
# Check GPU visibility
nvidia-smi

# Check Docker GPU access
docker run --rm --gpus all nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-base-ubuntu22.04 nvidia-smi

# Check Ollama GPU usage
docker exec ollama nvidia-smi

docs.title: GPU Not Visible in Container
docs.text:
Symptom: nvidia-smi works on host but not in container.

Fix:

# Install nvidia-container-toolkit
sudo apt install nvidia-container-toolkit
sudo systemctl restart docker

docs.title: OOM (Out of Memory)
docs.text:
Symptom: Model inference crashes with CUDA OOM error.

Fix:

  1. Reduce loaded models: 

    # In docker-compose.yml
OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS=2

  2. Use smaller quantization: 

    docker exec ollama ollama pull qwen3.5:9b:q4_K_M

  3. Restart Ollama to clear VRAM: 

    docker compose restart ollama

docs.title: Slow Inference
docs.text:
Symptom: Token generation much slower than expected.

Check:

  1. Verify Flash Attention: OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1
  2. Check VRAM pressure: nvidia-smi — VRAM near 100%?
  3. Check thermal throttling: nvidia-smi -q -d TEMPERATURE
  4. Ensure no background GPU tasks: close other GPU apps

docs.title: ComfyUI VRAM Conflict
docs.text:
Symptom: Image generation fails while Ollama models are loaded.

Fix: ComfyUI and Ollama share the GPU. When ComfyUI needs VRAM:

  1. Ollama will auto-unload idle models
  2. Reduce OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=5m for faster unloading
  3. Or use queue limits in the Dispatcher to prevent concurrent access

docs.title: Log Analysis
docs.text: Query and analyze logs using Loki and Grafana.

docs.title: Accessing Logs
docs.text: Open Grafana at http://192.168.2.103:3001, select the Loki data source, and use the Explore tab.

docs.title: All Errors
docs.text: {container_name="agent-runtime"} |= "ERROR"

docs.title: Intent Classification
docs.text: {container_name="agent-runtime"} |= "Intent classified"

docs.title: MarsRL Failures
docs.text: {container_name="agent-runtime"} |= "Verifier" |= "FAIL"

docs.title: Ollama Errors
docs.text: {container_name=~"ollama.*"} | json | level="error"

docs.title: Specific Session
docs.text: {container_name="agent-runtime"} |= "session=abc123"

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Use time range filters to narrow results
  • Pipe through | json for structured log parsing
  • Use | line_format for custom output formatting
  • Export results for offline analysis

docs.title: Onboard a User
docs.text: Set up a new user with access to Agent Swarm.

docs.title: 1. Provide Access URL
docs.text:
Give the user the Hive UI URL:

  • LAN: http://192.168.2.103/
  • Remote: Share Tailscale URL

docs.title: 2. Create Owner ID
docs.text:
Assign a unique owner_id for the user. This is used to scope:

  • Session history
  • Skills Memory rules
  • Training data
  • Preferences

docs.title: 3. Orient the User
docs.text:
Share these documentation pages:

  1. Getting Started: User Quickstart
  2. User Guide: Chat
  3. User Guide: Settings

docs.title: 4. Set Initial Preferences
docs.text:
The user can teach preferences via chat:

"Remember that I prefer detailed code comments" "I like responses in bullet point format"

These are stored in Skills Memory under their owner_id.

docs.title: 5. Verify Access
docs.text: Have the user send a test message through the Hive UI to confirm end-to-end functionality.

docs.title: Performance Tuning
docs.text: Optimize inference speed and system throughput.

docs.title: Flash Attention
docs.text:
Ensure Flash Attention is enabled:

OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1

docs.title: Concurrent Requests
docs.text:
Balance parallelism with VRAM:

Setting Low VRAM (8 GB) Medium (16 GB) High (24 GB+) OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL 1 2 4 OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS 1 3 5

docs.title: Context Window
docs.text:
Smaller context windows reduce VRAM and improve speed:

# In Modelfile
PARAMETER num_ctx 4096   # instead of 32768

docs.title: Stream Timeout
docs.text:
Increase for large responses:

STREAM_TIMEOUT = 120.0  # seconds

docs.title: MarsRL Tuning
docs.text:
For faster responses at the cost of quality verification:

Setting Faster Safer max_iter 1 2 pass_threshold 0.50 0.70

docs.title: Docker Resource Limits
docs.text:
services:
  agent-runtime:
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 8G
          cpus: "4.0"

docs.title: Network Latency
docs.text:
• Verify LAN latency: ping 192.168.2.101 should be < 1ms
  • Use wired connections for GPU nodes (not WiFi)

docs.title: Benchmarking
docs.text:
# Simple latency test
time curl -s -X POST http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/chat/completions \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}], "stream": false}'

Target: < 5 seconds for simple chat responses.

docs.title: Rotate SPIRE Keys
docs.text: Regenerate SPIRE join tokens and re-attest agents.

docs.title: When to Rotate
docs.text:
• Regularly (quarterly recommended)
  • After a security incident
  • When adding/removing nodes

docs.title: 1. Generate New Tokens
docs.text:
On the Control Node:

# Token for Execution Node
docker compose exec spire-server \
    /opt/spire/bin/spire-server token generate \
    -spiffeID spiffe://home-ai-lab/execution-node \
    -ttl 3600

# Token for Gateway Node
docker compose exec spire-server \
    /opt/spire/bin/spire-server token generate \
    -spiffeID spiffe://home-ai-lab/turing-gateway \
    -ttl 3600

Save both tokens.

docs.title: 2. Update Execution Node
docs.text:
# Stop SPIRE agent
docker compose stop spire-agent

# Update join token in agent.conf
nano execution_plane/config/spire/agent.conf
# Set: join_token = "<new-token>"

# Restart
docker compose up -d spire-agent

docs.title: 3. Update Gateway Node
docs.text:
# Same process on Turing
docker compose stop spire-agent
nano turing_gateway/config/spire/agent.conf
docker compose up -d spire-agent

docs.title: 4. Verify
docs.text:
# Check both agents
docker compose exec spire-agent /opt/spire/bin/spire-agent healthcheck

Both agents should report healthy.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Join tokens are single-use — each agent needs its own token
  • Tokens expire after TTL — use them promptly
  • Existing SVIDs remain valid until their TTL expires

docs.title: Switch Models
docs.text: Change the solver, router, or verifier model used by Agent Swarm.

docs.title: 1. Pull the New Model
docs.text: docker exec ollama ollama pull <new-model>

docs.title: 2. Update Environment
docs.text:
Edit network.env:

# Change from:
SOLVER_MODEL=qwen3.5:9b
# To:
SOLVER_MODEL=qwen3.5:14b

docs.title: 3. Restart Agent Runtime
docs.text:
cd execution_plane
docker compose restart agent-runtime

docs.title: 4. Verify
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/chat/completions \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What model are you using?"}],
        "stream": false
    }'

Check the response metadata for the new model name.

docs.title: 5. Monitor
docs.text:
Watch Langfuse traces and Grafana dashboards for:

  • Response quality changes
  • Latency differences
  • Error rate changes

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
If the new model performs poorly:

  1. Revert network.env to the previous model
  2. Restart Agent Runtime: docker compose restart agent-runtime
  3. The previous model should still be cached in Ollama

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
Supplementary reference material for Agent Swarm.

  • Glossary — definitions of terms and acronyms
  • Environment Variables — complete variable reference
  • Changelog — release history
  • Contributing — how to contribute
