docs.title: Changelog
docs.text: All notable changes to Agent Swarm are documented here.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Comprehensive MkDocs documentation site
  • Docker-based documentation deployment
  • GitHub Pages deployment via GitHub Actions

docs.title: Changed
docs.text: • Documentation restructured as a full library of procedures, tutorials, and references

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Multi-agent orchestration (Router → Coordinator → Solver)
  • MarsRL verification loop (solve → verify → reward)
  • Image generation via ComfyUI integration
  • 3D model generation pipeline
  • Voice interface (Whisper STT + Piper TTS)
  • IoT control via Home Assistant
  • Research Mode with web search
  • Skills Memory (PostgreSQL + pgvector)
  • SPIRE-based service identity and mTLS
  • Langfuse observability and tracing
  • Prometheus + Grafana monitoring
  • Loki log aggregation
  • Traefik reverse proxy and load balancing
  • GRPO preference training
  • Governance system for sensitive operations
  • Hive UI web interface
  • GPU allocation and management
  • 3-node distributed architecture

docs.title: Infrastructure
docs.text:
• Docker Compose deployment across 3 nodes
  • Control Plane: SPIRE server, PostgreSQL, Langfuse
  • Execution Plane: Ollama, Agent Runtime, ComfyUI, Voice
  • Gateway: Traefik, Prometheus, Grafana, Loki

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to Agent Swarm.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository
  2. Clone your fork
  3. Set up a local development environment
  4. Create a feature branch: git checkout -b feature/my-feature

docs.title: Development Workflow
docs.text:
1. Make your changes
  2. Write or update tests
  3. Run tests: pytest
  4. Commit with a clear message: git commit -m "Add: my new feature"
  5. Push to your fork: git push origin feature/my-feature
  6. Open a Pull Request

docs.title: Code Standards
docs.text:
• Python: follow PEP 8
  • Use type hints for function signatures
  • Add docstrings for public functions and classes
  • Log errors with contextual information

docs.title: Pull Request Guidelines
docs.text:
• One feature/fix per PR
  • Include a clear description of what and why
  • Reference related issues
  • Ensure all tests pass
  • Keep PRs focused and reasonably sized

docs.title: Areas for Contribution
docs.text:
• New Agents — see Adding Agents
  • New Tools — see Adding Tools
  • Documentation — fix typos, improve clarity, add examples
  • Bug Fixes — check open issues
  • Tests — improve coverage

docs.title: Reporting Issues
docs.text:
Open a GitHub issue with:

  • Clear description of the problem
  • Steps to reproduce
  • Expected vs. actual behavior
  • Logs or error messages
  • Environment details (OS, GPU, Docker version)

docs.title: Documentation Contributions
docs.text:
Documentation lives in docs-site/docs/. To preview:

cd docs-site
pip install -r requirements.txt
mkdocs serve

Then open http://localhost:8000.

docs.title: Environment Variables Reference
docs.text: Complete reference for all environment variables used in network.env.

docs.title: Model Configuration
docs.text: Variable Description Default SOLVER_MODEL Primary response generation model qwen3.5:9b ROUTER_MODEL Intent classification model nemotron-orchestrator:8b VERIFIER_MODEL Safety/quality verification model llama-guard-3:8b

docs.title: Ollama
docs.text: Variable Description Default OLLAMA_HOST Ollama server address 192.168.2.101 OLLAMA_PORT Ollama API port 11434 OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION Enable Flash Attention 1 OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL Max concurrent requests 4 OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS Max models in VRAM 3 OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE Idle model unload timeout 10m

docs.title: Node Addresses
docs.text: Variable Description Default HOPPER_IP Control Plane address 192.168.2.102 LOVELACE_IP Execution Plane address 192.168.2.101 TURING_IP Gateway / Turing address 192.168.2.103

docs.title: Langfuse
docs.text: Variable Description LANGFUSE_HOST Langfuse server URL LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY Langfuse public API key LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY Langfuse secret key

docs.title: PostgreSQL
docs.text: Variable Description POSTGRES_HOST PostgreSQL server address POSTGRES_PORT PostgreSQL port (default: 5432) POSTGRES_USER Database username POSTGRES_PASSWORD Database password POSTGRES_DB Database name

docs.title: Home Assistant
docs.text: Variable Description HA_URL Home Assistant API URL HA_TOKEN Long-lived access token

docs.title: SPIRE
docs.text: Variable Description SPIRE_SERVER_ADDRESS SPIRE server endpoint SPIRE_TRUST_DOMAIN Trust domain (e.g., home-ai-lab)

docs.title: ComfyUI
docs.text: Variable Description Default COMFYUI_HOST ComfyUI server address 192.168.2.101 COMFYUI_PORT ComfyUI API port 8188

docs.title: Agent Runtime
docs.text:
Variable Description Default AGENT_RUNTIME_PORT API listen port 8008 STREAM_TIMEOUT SSE stream timeout (seconds) 120 LOG_LEVEL Logging level INFO

Security

Never commit network.env to version control. It contains secrets. See Secrets Management.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Term Definition Action Figure Pipeline Specialized 3D generation workflow optimized for character models Agent A specialized component that handles a specific category of tasks (e.g., Image Agent, IoT Agent) Agent Runtime The main Python application that hosts all agents, the router, coordinator, and tools ComfyUI Node-based image/video generation tool used for AI art and 3D reference images Control Node The server (192.168.2.102) running SPIRE, PostgreSQL, and Langfuse Coordinator The orchestration component that manages agent dispatch and multi-step workflows Dispatcher Task queue manager that routes jobs to appropriate agents Execution Node The server (192.168.2.101) running Ollama, Agent Runtime, ComfyUI, and voice services Gateway Node The Turing server (192.168.2.103) running Traefik, Prometheus, Grafana, and Loki GRPO Group Relative Policy Optimization — the preference training algorithm used by MarsRL Hive UI The web-based chat interface for interacting with Agent Swarm Intent The classified purpose of a user message (e.g., general_chat, image_generation, iot_control) Langfuse Open-source LLM observability platform used for tracing and analytics Loki Log aggregation system by Grafana Labs MarsRL Mars Reinforcement Learning — the solve → verify → reward loop that ensures response quality MemPalace The vector memory system using PostgreSQL + pgvector for semantic retrieval mTLS Mutual TLS — both client and server authenticate each other with certificates Ollama Local LLM inference server that runs models on GPU pgvector PostgreSQL extension for vector similarity search, used for embeddings Piper Local text-to-speech (TTS) engine Prometheus Metrics collection and alerting system Router The intent classification component that determines which agent handles a request Skills Memory Long-term memory system that stores user preferences and learned patterns Solver The primary LLM model that generates responses to user queries SPIFFE Secure Production Identity Framework for Everyone — standard for service identity SPIRE SPIFFE Runtime Environment — provides cryptographic identity (SVIDs) to services SVID SPIFFE Verifiable Identity Document — a short-lived X.509 certificate Traefik Cloud-native reverse proxy and load balancer Verifier The LLM model that checks response quality and safety before delivery VRAM Video RAM — GPU memory used for model inference Whisper OpenAI's speech-to-text (STT) model Workflow A ComfyUI node graph defining an image/video generation pipeline

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Symptom-based guides for diagnosing and resolving issues. Each page covers a specific component.

Component Common Issues Ollama Model load failures, OOM, slow inference ComfyUI Workflow errors, VRAM conflicts SPIRE Attestation failures, expired SVIDs Network Connectivity, DNS, Traefik routing GPU Driver issues, VRAM, thermal throttling Agent Runtime Crashes, timeouts, intent routing Langfuse Tracing failures, database connection Docker Container failures, disk space, networking Voice STT/TTS failures, audio issues

Tip

For quick answers, try the Troubleshooting FAQ first.

docs.title: Runtime Won't Start
docs.text:
Symptom: Container exits immediately or fails health check.

Diagnose:

docker logs agent-runtime --tail 50

Common causes:

  • Missing environment variables in network.env
  • Port conflict on 8000
  • Python import errors (missing dependencies)

Fix:

  • Verify network.env has all required variables
  • Rebuild the image: docker compose build agent-runtime
  • Check for syntax errors in agent code

docs.title: Intent Misclassification
docs.text:
Symptom: Messages routed to wrong agent (e.g., "draw a cat" goes to general chat).

Diagnose:

Check Langfuse traces for the intent classification scores.

Fix:

  1. Add better examples to agents/intent_capabilities.py
  2. Try a larger router model
  3. Adjust confidence thresholds in the Router

docs.title: Response Timeout
docs.text:
Symptom: Chat hangs or returns timeout error.

Diagnose:

# Check if Ollama is responsive
curl http://192.168.2.101:11434/api/tags

# Check agent runtime logs
docker logs agent-runtime --tail 20

Fix:

  1. Increase STREAM_TIMEOUT in agent configuration
  2. Check Ollama health and restart if needed
  3. A very large context may cause slow responses — reduce history

docs.title: MarsRL Verification Loop
docs.text:
Symptom: Response takes very long, logs show multiple "Verifier: FAIL" entries.

Fix:

  1. Reduce max_iter in MarsRL config (try 1 instead of 2)
  2. Lower pass_threshold (try 0.50 instead of 0.70)
  3. Check if the verifier model is the right size for the task

docs.title: Memory/Context Issues
docs.text:
Symptom: Agent doesn't remember previous messages in conversation.

Check:

  • Session ID is being passed correctly
  • Context window isn't exceeded
  • Memory system (PostgreSQL + pgvector) is healthy

docs.title: Workflow Execution Fails
docs.text:
Symptom: Image generation returns an error.

Diagnose:

# Check ComfyUI health
curl http://192.168.2.101:8188/system_stats

# Check logs
docker logs comfyui --tail 50

Fix:

  • Verify the workflow JSON is valid
  • Check that all required custom nodes are installed
  • Restart ComfyUI: docker compose restart comfyui

docs.title: VRAM Conflict with Ollama
docs.text:
Symptom: ComfyUI fails with OOM when Ollama models are loaded.

Fix:

  1. Set OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=5m for faster model unloading
  2. Wait for Ollama models to unload before generating images
  3. Reduce OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS=1 to free VRAM faster

docs.title: Missing Custom Nodes
docs.text:
Symptom: Workflow references nodes that don't exist.

Diagnose:

Check the ComfyUI log for "Node type not found" errors.

Fix:

# Install missing nodes
docker exec comfyui pip install <package>
# Or clone into custom_nodes/
docker exec comfyui git clone <repo> /comfyui/custom_nodes/<name>
docker compose restart comfyui

docs.title: Slow Image Generation
docs.text:
Symptom: Images take much longer than expected.

Check:

  1. GPU utilization during generation: nvidia-smi
  2. Image resolution — larger = slower
  3. Number of sampling steps — more = slower
  4. Is Ollama competing for VRAM?

docs.title: ComfyUI Web Interface Not Loading
docs.text:
Symptom: http://192.168.2.101:8188 doesn't respond.

Fix:

docker compose ps comfyui  # Check status
docker compose restart comfyui
docker logs comfyui --tail 20

docs.title: Container Won't Start
docs.text:
Symptom: Container exits immediately after starting.

Diagnose:

docker logs <container-name> --tail 50

docker inspect <container-name> --format='{{.State.ExitCode}}'

Common causes:

  • Missing environment variables
  • Port already in use
  • Volume mount permission issues
  • Image not built

docs.title: Disk Space Full
docs.text:
Symptom: Containers fail to start or operations fail with I/O errors.

Diagnose:

df -h
docker system df

Fix:

# Remove unused images, containers, volumes
docker system prune -a --volumes

# Remove old build caches
docker builder prune

Warning

docker system prune --volumes removes unused volumes. Make sure your data is backed up first.

docs.title: Network Connectivity Between Containers
docs.text:
Symptom: One container can't reach another.

Diagnose:

# Check networks
docker network ls
docker network inspect <network-name>

# Test from inside a container
docker exec <container> ping <other-container>

Fix:

  • Ensure both containers are on the same Docker network
  • Check Docker Compose networks: configuration
  • Use service names (not container names) for inter-container DNS

docs.title: Permission Denied on Volumes
docs.text:
Symptom: Container logs show permission errors on mounted volumes.

Fix:

# Fix ownership
sudo chown -R 1000:1000 /path/to/volume

# Or use user mapping in docker-compose
user: "1000:1000"

docs.title: Docker Compose Version Issues
docs.text:
Symptom: docker compose commands fail with syntax errors.

Fix:

  • Use docker compose (v2, built-in) not docker-compose (v1, standalone)
  • Verify version: docker compose version
  • Update Docker Engine for the latest Compose

docs.title: GPU Not Detected
docs.text:
Symptom: nvidia-smi fails or shows no GPU.

Fix:

  1. Install/update NVIDIA driver: 
    sudo apt install nvidia-driver-550
sudo reboot
  2. Verify: nvidia-smi

docs.title: GPU Not Available in Docker
docs.text:
Symptom: nvidia-smi works on host but not in containers.

Fix:

# Install NVIDIA Container Toolkit
curl -fsSL https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/gpgkey | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg
curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/stable/deb/nvidia-container-toolkit.list | \
    sed 's#deb https://#deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg] https://#g' | \
    sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/nvidia-container-toolkit.list
sudo apt update
sudo apt install nvidia-container-toolkit
sudo nvidia-ctk runtime configure --runtime=docker
sudo systemctl restart docker

Test: docker run --rm --gpus all nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-base-ubuntu22.04 nvidia-smi

docs.title: Thermal Throttling
docs.text:
Symptom: Performance degrades after sustained workloads.

Diagnose:

nvidia-smi -q -d TEMPERATURE

Fix:

  • Improve case airflow
  • Set fan curve to aggressive
  • Add thermal pads/paste if needed
  • Reduce sustained workload intensity

docs.title: Multi-GPU Selection
docs.text:
Symptom: Wrong GPU is being used.

Fix:

Set CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES in docker-compose:

environment:
  - CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0  # Or 1, 2, etc.

docs.title: Driver/CUDA Version Mismatch
docs.text:
Symptom: CUDA errors or library version conflicts.

Check:

nvidia-smi  # Shows driver and max CUDA version
nvcc --version  # Shows installed CUDA toolkit version

Ensure the Docker images use a compatible CUDA version.

docs.title: Langfuse Dashboard Not Loading
docs.text:
Symptom: http://192.168.2.102:3000 doesn't respond.

Diagnose:

docker compose ps langfuse
docker logs langfuse --tail 30

Fix:

  • Check PostgreSQL is running (Langfuse depends on it)
  • Verify LANGFUSE_DATABASE_URL in environment
  • Restart: docker compose restart langfuse

docs.title: Traces Not Appearing
docs.text:
Symptom: Conversations happen but no traces in Langfuse.

Diagnose:

Check agent runtime logs for Langfuse connection errors.

Fix:

  1. Verify LANGFUSE_HOST, LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY, LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY in network.env
  2. Test connectivity from agent runtime: curl http://192.168.2.102:3000/api/public/health
  3. Check that the Langfuse SDK is initialized in the agent code

docs.title: Database Connection Errors
docs.text:
Symptom: Langfuse logs show PostgreSQL connection refused.

Fix:

  1. Verify PostgreSQL is running: docker compose ps postgres
  2. Check credentials in Langfuse environment variables
  3. Verify the database exists: 
    docker compose exec postgres psql -U postgres -l

docs.title: High Disk Usage
docs.text:
Symptom: PostgreSQL database growing very large from traces.

Fix:

  1. Set a retention policy in Langfuse settings
  2. Archive old traces
  3. Vacuum the database: 
    docker compose exec postgres psql -U postgres -c "VACUUM FULL;"

docs.title: Traefik Not Routing
docs.text:
Symptom: Requests to 192.168.2.103 return 404 or timeout.

Diagnose:

# Check Traefik dashboard
curl http://192.168.2.103:8080/api/overview

# Check active routers
curl http://192.168.2.103:8080/api/http/routers | python -m json.tool

Fix:

  1. Verify Docker labels on the target service
  2. Check that the service is on the correct Docker network
  3. Restart Traefik: docker compose restart traefik

docs.title: Service Unreachable Between Nodes
docs.text:
Symptom: One node can't reach a service on another node.

Diagnose:

# Basic connectivity
ping 192.168.2.101

# Port check
curl -v http://192.168.2.101:11434/api/tags

Fix:

  1. Check firewall rules: sudo ufw status or sudo iptables -L
  2. Verify both nodes are on the same subnet
  3. Check Docker network configuration

docs.title: DNS Resolution Failures
docs.text:
Symptom: Services can't resolve hostnames.

Fix:

  • Use IP addresses instead of hostnames in network.env
  • Check Docker's DNS: docker exec <container> cat /etc/resolv.conf
  • Add static entries if needed: extra_hosts in docker-compose

docs.title: Tailscale VPN Issues
docs.text:
Symptom: Remote access via Tailscale doesn't work.

Fix:

  1. Check Tailscale status: tailscale status
  2. Verify the node is online in Tailscale admin
  3. Check that services listen on 0.0.0.0 (not 127.0.0.1)
  4. Verify Tailscale IP routing

docs.title: Port Conflicts
docs.text:
Symptom: Container fails to start with "port already in use".

Diagnose:

sudo ss -tlnp | grep <port>

Fix:

  • Stop the conflicting process
  • Or remap the port in docker-compose.yml
  • See Port Map for all used ports

docs.title: Model Fails to Load
docs.text:
Symptom: Error like model not found or failed to load model.

Diagnose:

# List available models
docker exec ollama ollama list

# Check Ollama logs
docker logs ollama --tail 50

Fix:

  • Pull the model: docker exec ollama ollama pull <model>
  • Check disk space: docker exec ollama df -h /root/.ollama

docs.title: Out of Memory (OOM)
docs.text:
Symptom: CUDA out of memory or model loading stalls.

Diagnose:

docker exec ollama nvidia-smi

Fix:

  1. Reduce loaded models: OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS=2
  2. Use smaller quantizations (q4_K_M instead of q8_0)
  3. Reduce context window: OLLAMA_NUM_CTX=4096
  4. Restart Ollama: docker compose restart ollama

docs.title: Slow Inference
docs.text:
Symptom: Tokens per second much lower than expected.

Diagnose:

# Check GPU utilization
docker exec ollama nvidia-smi -l 1

# Check if Flash Attention is enabled
docker inspect ollama | grep FLASH

Fix:

  1. Enable Flash Attention: OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1
  2. Reduce OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL if VRAM is constrained
  3. Check for thermal throttling: nvidia-smi -q -d TEMPERATURE
  4. Ensure no other GPU processes are running

docs.title: Ollama Won't Start
docs.text:
Symptom: Container exits immediately.

Diagnose:

docker logs ollama

Fix:

  • Check NVIDIA Container Toolkit: docker run --rm --gpus all nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-base-ubuntu22.04 nvidia-smi
  • Verify GPU runtime: ensure runtime: nvidia is in docker-compose
  • Check port conflicts: ensure port 11434 is free

docs.title: Model Corruption
docs.text:
Symptom: Model loads but produces garbage output.

Fix:

docker exec ollama ollama rm <model>
docker exec ollama ollama pull <model>

docs.title: Agent Attestation Fails
docs.text:
Symptom: SPIRE agent can't connect to server. Log shows attestation errors.

Diagnose:

docker compose exec spire-agent /opt/spire/bin/spire-agent healthcheck
docker logs spire-agent --tail 30

Fix:

  1. Generate a fresh join token on the Control Node: 
    docker compose exec spire-server /opt/spire/bin/spire-server token generate \
    -spiffeID spiffe://home-ai-lab/execution-node -ttl 3600
  2. Update the agent configuration with the new token
  3. Restart the agent: docker compose restart spire-agent

docs.title: Expired SVIDs
docs.text:
Symptom: Service-to-service calls fail with TLS certificate errors.

Diagnose:

docker compose exec spire-agent /opt/spire/bin/spire-agent api fetch x509

Check the expiry timestamp.

Fix:

  • SVIDs auto-rotate. If they've stopped rotating, the agent may have lost connection to the server
  • Re-attest the agent with a new join token
  • Verify SPIRE server is healthy: docker compose exec spire-server /opt/spire/bin/spire-server healthcheck

docs.title: SPIRE Server Unreachable
docs.text:
Symptom: Agent logs show "connection refused" to server.

Fix:

  1. Verify the server is running: docker compose ps spire-server
  2. Check network connectivity: ping 192.168.2.102
  3. Verify the port (8081) is accessible
  4. Check firewall rules

docs.title: Registration Entry Issues
docs.text:
Symptom: Agent is attested but SVIDs don't have the right SPIFFE IDs.

Check:

docker compose exec spire-server /opt/spire/bin/spire-server entry show

Verify entries exist for each service with the correct selectors.

docs.title: Speech-to-Text (STT) Not Working
docs.text:
Symptom: Microphone records but no transcription appears.

Diagnose:

# Check Whisper service
curl http://192.168.2.101:9000/health
docker logs whisper --tail 30

Fix:

  1. Restart Whisper: docker compose restart whisper
  2. Check that the Whisper model is loaded
  3. Verify audio format compatibility (16kHz WAV preferred)

docs.title: Text-to-Speech (TTS) Not Working
docs.text:
Symptom: No audio plays after responses.

Diagnose:

# Check Piper service
curl http://192.168.2.101:5500/health
docker logs piper --tail 30

Fix:

  1. Check browser audio permissions
  2. Verify Piper voice model is installed
  3. Restart Piper: docker compose restart piper

docs.title: Poor Transcription Quality
docs.text:
Symptom: Whisper transcribes words incorrectly.

Fix:

  1. Reduce background noise
  2. Speak clearly and at normal pace
  3. Use a better microphone
  4. Try a larger Whisper model (e.g., medium instead of base)

docs.title: Audio Latency
docs.text:
Symptom: Long delay between speaking and transcription.

Fix:

  1. Use a smaller Whisper model for faster processing
  2. Check CPU/GPU load during transcription
  3. Reduce audio chunk size for faster streaming

docs.title: Browser Microphone Issues
docs.text:
Symptom: "Microphone not found" or permission denied.

Fix:

  1. Check browser settings → Site Settings → Microphone
  2. Ensure the page is served over HTTPS (or localhost)
  3. Try a different browser
  4. Check OS audio input settings

docs.text:
Hands-on, narrative walkthroughs that guide you through complete tasks from start to finish.

Prerequisites

All tutorials assume you have a running Agent Swarm deployment. If not, start with the Getting Started guide.

docs.title: Build a 3D Model
docs.text: Generate 3D assets from text descriptions.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to trigger 3D generation
  • The multi-stage 3D pipeline
  • How to iterate on results

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• ComfyUI running with 3D-capable models
  • Sufficient GPU VRAM (16 GB+ recommended)

docs.title: Step 1: Describe Your Object
docs.text:
In the Hive UI:

Generate a 3D model of a medieval sword

The Router classifies this as 3d_generation and routes to the 3D Pipeline agent.

docs.title: Step 2: Understand the Pipeline
docs.text:
The 3D generation pipeline follows multiple stages:

  1. Prompt Enhancement — the solver enriches your description
  2. Reference Image — ComfyUI generates a reference image
  3. Multi-View Generation — creates views from multiple angles
  4. Mesh Reconstruction — builds 3D geometry from the views
  5. Texture Mapping — applies textures to the mesh

docs.title: Step 3: Review the Output
docs.text:
The agent returns:

  • A preview render of the 3D model
  • Download link for the mesh file (.obj or .glb)
  • The enhanced prompt used

docs.title: Step 4: Iterate
docs.text:
Refine your model:

Make the sword blade more curved and add runes on the blade

The pipeline re-runs with the adjusted description.

docs.title: Step 5: Action Figure Mode
docs.text:
For character models, use the Action Figure pipeline:

Create an action figure of a robot samurai

This uses a specialized workflow optimized for character models with proper proportions and articulation.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Start simple: basic shapes before complex scenes
  • Specify material: "metallic", "wooden", "glass"
  • Specify style: "realistic", "low-poly", "cartoon"
  • Single objects work better than full scenes

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• User Guide: 3D Generation — full reference
  • Modules: 3D Pipeline — technical details

docs.title: Create a Custom Agent
docs.text: Build, register, and deploy your own specialized agent.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• The agent interface and registration API
  • How to implement custom logic
  • How to integrate with existing tools

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Development environment set up (see Local Development)
  • Understanding of the agent architecture (see Agent System)

docs.title: Step 1: Create the Agent File
docs.text:
Create agents/my_agent.py:

from agents.coordinator import Coordinator
from agents.logger_setup import get_logger

logger = get_logger("my_agent")


class MyAgent:
    """A custom agent that handles a specific domain."""

    def __init__(self):
        self.name = "my_agent"
        self.description = "Handles custom-domain tasks"

    async def handle(self, message: str, context: dict) -> str:
        """Process a user message and return a response."""
        logger.info(f"MyAgent handling: {message[:50]}...")

        # Your custom logic here
        # Use context for session info, owner_id, etc.

        result = await self._process(message, context)
        return result

    async def _process(self, message: str, context: dict) -> str:
        # Implement your logic
        return f"MyAgent processed: {message}"

docs.title: Step 2: Register the Intent
docs.text:
Add your agent's intent to the Router's capability map in agents/intent_capabilities.py:

INTENT_MAP = {
    # ... existing intents ...
    "my_domain": {
        "agent": "my_agent",
        "description": "Handles custom-domain tasks",
        "examples": [
            "do my custom thing",
            "handle this special request",
        ],
    },
}

docs.title: Step 3: Register with the Coordinator
docs.text:
In agents/coordinator.py, register your agent:

from agents.my_agent import MyAgent

# In the initialization
self.agents["my_agent"] = MyAgent()

docs.title: Step 4: Add Tools (Optional)
docs.text:
If your agent needs tools, create them in core_tools/:

# core_tools/my_tool.py
class MyTool:
    name = "my_tool"
    description = "Does a specific thing"

    async def execute(self, params: dict) -> dict:
        # Tool logic
        return {"result": "done"}

docs.title: Step 5: Test
docs.text:
# Send a test message
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "do my custom thing"}]
    }'

Verify your agent handles the request by checking logs and Langfuse traces.

docs.title: Step 6: Deploy
docs.text:
Rebuild and restart the agent runtime:

cd execution_plane
docker compose build agent-runtime
docker compose up -d agent-runtime

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Developer Guide: Adding Agents — detailed guide
  • Developer Guide: Adding Tools — tool development

docs.title: Your First Chat
docs.text: Send your first message to Agent Swarm and understand how it processes your request.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to access the chat interface
  • How messages flow through the system
  • How to interpret responses

docs.title: Step 1: Open the Chat Interface
docs.text:
Navigate to the Hive UI:

http://192.168.2.103/

You'll see the chat interface with a text input at the bottom.

docs.title: Step 2: Send a Message
docs.text:
Type a simple question:

What can you help me with?

Press Enter or click the send button.

docs.title: Step 3: Watch the Flow
docs.text:
Behind the scenes, your message flows through:

  1. Hive UI → sends to Agent Runtime API
  2. Router (nemotron-orchestrator:8b) → classifies your intent as general_chat
  3. Coordinator → dispatches to the solver
  4. Solver (qwen3.5:9b) → generates a response
  5. MarsRL Verifier (llama-guard-3:8b) → checks safety and quality
  6. Response streams back to the UI

You should see a streaming response appear in real-time.

docs.title: Step 4: Try Different Intents
docs.text:
Each of these triggers a different agent:

Message Intent Agent "What's 2+2?" general_chat Solver "Draw a sunset" image_generation Image Agent "Turn on the lights" iot_control IoT Agent "Generate a 3D model of a chair" 3d_generation 3D Pipeline

docs.title: Step 5: Check the Trace
docs.text:
Open Langfuse at http://192.168.2.102:3000 to see the full trace of your conversation, including:

  • Intent classification scores
  • Model latency
  • Token usage
  • Verifier results

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Generate an Image — try creative generation
  • User Guide: Chat — full chat feature reference

docs.title: Generate an Image
docs.text: Create AI-generated images using natural language prompts.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to trigger the Image Agent
  • How prompts are enhanced
  • How to adjust style and quality

docs.title: Step 1: Open the Chat
docs.text: Navigate to http://192.168.2.103/ and open a new conversation.

docs.title: Step 2: Describe Your Image
docs.text:
Type a natural language description:

Create a watercolor painting of a cozy mountain cabin at sunset

The Router detects image_generation intent and routes to the Image Agent.

docs.title: Step 3: Understand the Pipeline
docs.text:
The Image Agent:

  1. Enhances your prompt using the solver model to add artistic details
  2. Selects a ComfyUI workflow (e.g., workflow_hunyuan_paint.json)
  3. Queues the job on ComfyUI at 192.168.2.101:8188
  4. Returns the image in the chat

docs.title: Step 4: Iterate
docs.text:
Refine your image with follow-up messages:

Make it more vibrant with autumn colors

The agent maintains context and adjusts the prompt.

docs.title: Step 5: Advanced Prompting
docs.text:
For more control, specify:

  • Style: "in the style of Studio Ghibli"
  • Medium: "oil painting", "digital art", "photograph"
  • Composition: "close-up", "wide angle", "bird's eye view"
  • Lighting: "golden hour", "dramatic lighting", "soft ambient"

docs.title: Example Gallery
docs.text: Prompt Description "A cyberpunk city at night, neon lights, rain" Sci-fi cityscape "Portrait of a cat wearing a crown, oil painting" Stylized pet portrait "Minimalist logo design for a coffee shop" Design work

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:

  • Build a 3D Model — go from 2D to 3D

docs.title: Create IoT Automation
docs.text: Control smart home devices through natural language chat commands.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How Agent Swarm connects to Home Assistant
  • How to issue device commands via chat
  • How to create automation routines

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Home Assistant running and accessible
  • Devices configured in Home Assistant
  • HA_URL and HA_TOKEN set in network.env

docs.title: Step 1: Verify Connection
docs.text:
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $HA_TOKEN" \
    http://<ha-ip>:8123/api/states | python -m json.tool | head -20

You should see a list of entity states.

docs.title: Step 2: Simple Commands
docs.text:
In the Hive UI, try:

Turn on the living room lights

The Router classifies this as iot_control and routes to the IoT Agent, which calls the Home Assistant API.

docs.title: Step 3: Query Device State
docs.text:
What's the temperature in the bedroom?

The IoT Agent queries the relevant sensor entity and responds with the current value.

docs.title: Step 4: Complex Commands
docs.text:
Set the living room lights to 50% brightness and warm white

The agent translates this into the appropriate Home Assistant service call with brightness and color temperature parameters.

docs.title: Step 5: Automations via Chat
docs.text:
Every weekday at 7am, turn on the kitchen lights and set the thermostat to 72

This creates a Home Assistant automation through the API.

docs.title: Example Commands
docs.text: Command What Happens "Turn off all lights" Calls light.turn_off for all light entities "Lock the front door" Calls lock.lock for the door lock entity "Is the garage door open?" Queries the garage door cover entity state "Set thermostat to 68" Calls climate.set_temperature

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• User Guide: IoT Control — full IoT reference
  • Modules: Home Assistant Tool — technical details

docs.title: Multi-Agent Workflow
docs.text: Orchestrate multiple agents to handle complex, multi-step tasks.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How agents collaborate on complex requests
  • How the Coordinator manages agent handoffs
  • How to craft prompts that trigger multi-agent flows

docs.title: Step 1: Understand Agent Collaboration
docs.text:
When a task requires multiple capabilities, the Coordinator orchestrates:

sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant C as Coordinator
    participant S as Solver
    participant I as Image Agent
    participant F as File Ops

    U->>C: "Write a blog post about AI and include an illustration"
    C->>S: Generate blog post text
    S-->>C: Blog post content
    C->>I: Generate illustration
    I-->>C: Image
    C->>F: Save both to workspace
    F-->>C: Done
    C-->>U: Blog post + image saved

docs.title: Step 2: Try a Multi-Step Request
docs.text:
Research the history of neural networks, write a summary, and create an infographic showing the timeline

This triggers:

  1. Search Tool — gathers research material
  2. Solver — writes the summary
  3. Image Agent — creates the timeline infographic

docs.title: Step 3: Code + Documentation
docs.text:
Write a Python function to calculate Fibonacci numbers, add docstrings, and create a test file

This triggers:

  1. Solver — writes the function
  2. Solver — adds documentation
  3. File Ops — saves the code
  4. Solver — writes tests
  5. File Ops — saves the test file

docs.title: Step 4: IoT + Voice
docs.text:
When someone says "movie time", dim the living room lights to 20% and turn on the TV

This combines:

  1. IoT Agent — creates the automation
  2. Voice understanding — sets up the trigger phrase

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Be explicit about what you want: list each step
  • Use "and" or numbered lists to signal multi-step tasks
  • Complex workflows may take longer — watch the streaming output

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Architecture: Agent System — how agents interact
  • Modules: Coordinator — orchestration details

docs.title: Research a Topic
docs.text: Use Research Mode for deep-dive analysis with web search and multi-step reasoning.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to activate Research Mode
  • How the system gathers and synthesizes information
  • How to refine research results

docs.title: Step 1: Activate Research Mode
docs.text:
In the Hive UI, either:

  • Toggle the Research Mode switch, or
  • Prefix your message with a research-style query

docs.title: Step 2: Ask a Research Question
docs.text:
Research the latest developments in quantum computing error correction

The system will:

  1. Break down your question into sub-queries
  2. Search the web using the Search Tool
  3. Synthesize findings from multiple sources
  4. Cite sources in the response

docs.title: Step 3: Review Results
docs.text:
The response includes:

  • A structured summary
  • Key findings organized by theme
  • Source citations with links
  • Areas of uncertainty or debate

docs.title: Step 4: Go Deeper
docs.text:
Follow up with targeted questions:

Tell me more about surface codes vs. color codes

The agent maintains context from the initial research and provides more detailed analysis.

docs.title: Step 5: Export
docs.text:
Summarize your findings as a markdown report

The agent generates a structured document you can copy or save.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Be specific: "quantum computing error correction in 2024" vs. "quantum computing"
  • Ask for comparisons: "Compare X vs Y"
  • Request specific formats: "Give me a table comparing approaches"

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• User Guide: Research Mode — full reference
  • Modules: Search Tool — how search works

docs.title: Train Model Preferences
docs.text: Teach Agent Swarm your preferences using GRPO training.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to provide preference feedback
  • How feedback is collected and stored
  • How GRPO training improves responses

docs.title: Step 1: Interact Naturally
docs.text: Use the system normally. Over time, you'll notice responses you like and responses you'd change.

docs.title: Step 2: Provide Explicit Feedback
docs.text:
When you see a response you like or dislike:

  • Thumbs up — marks the response as preferred
  • Thumbs down — marks it as rejected
  • Edit and resubmit — teaches the correct response

docs.title: Step 3: Teach Preferences via Chat
docs.text:
You can also state preferences directly:

Remember that I prefer concise answers with code examples I like responses formatted as bullet points

These are stored in Skills Memory under your owner_id.

docs.title: Step 4: View Training Data
docs.text:
Training pairs are stored in training_data/:

{
    "prompt": "Explain Docker volumes",
    "chosen": "Docker volumes persist data...",
    "rejected": "Volumes in Docker are..."
}

docs.title: Step 5: Run GRPO Training
docs.text:
Admin Only

Training requires admin access.

# Trigger training
curl -X POST http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/training/trigger \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"owner_id": "user-123"}'

The MarsRL loop uses your preference data to fine-tune response quality.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[User Feedback] --> B[Training Data]
    B --> C[GRPO Optimizer]
    C --> D[Updated Reward Model]
    D --> E[Better Responses]
    E --> A

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• User Guide: Training — full training reference
  • Architecture: MarsRL — how MarsRL works

docs.title: Build a Voice Assistant
docs.text: Set up voice input and output to talk to Agent Swarm.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to enable voice input (STT)
  • How to enable voice output (TTS)
  • How voice integrates with the agent pipeline

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• A microphone connected to the device running the browser
  • Voice services enabled on the Execution Node

docs.title: Step 1: Verify Voice Services
docs.text:
# Check Whisper (STT) is running
curl http://192.168.2.101:9000/health

# Check Piper (TTS) is running
curl http://192.168.2.101:5500/health

docs.title: Step 2: Enable Voice in the UI
docs.text:
In the Hive UI (http://192.168.2.103/):

  1. Click the microphone icon in the chat input area
  2. Grant browser microphone permission when prompted
  3. Speak your message

The audio is sent to Whisper for transcription, then processed as text.

docs.title: Step 3: Enable Voice Output
docs.text:
Agent responses can be spoken aloud:

  1. Open Settings in the UI
  2. Enable Text-to-Speech
  3. Select a voice (Piper offers multiple voices)

Responses will be both displayed as text and played as audio.

docs.title: Step 4: Try a Conversation
docs.text:
Speak naturally:

"What's the weather like?" "Turn on the living room lights" "Tell me a joke"

The system handles the full loop: speech → text → agent → text → speech.

docs.title: Step 5: Adjust Voice Settings
docs.text: Setting Effect Voice model Changes the speaker voice Speed Adjusts speech rate Auto-listen Automatically starts listening after TTS finishes

docs.text: Issue Fix Microphone not detected Check browser permissions Poor transcription Reduce background noise, speak clearly No audio output Check browser audio permissions and volume

docs.title: 3D Generation
docs.text: Create 3D models, meshes, and articulated action figures for 3D printing.

docs.title: How to Access
docs.text:
• UI: Navigate to Art Studio → 3D tab, or use Chat
  • Chat: Describe what you want — the router detects 3D or ACTION_FIGURE intent

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
"Create a low-poly medieval shield with ornate carvings"

The system routes to the 3D generation pipeline, generates concept art, and passes it through the 3D reconstruction pipeline using TripoSG or Hunyuan3D.

docs.title: Pipeline
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Text Prompt] --> B[Concept Art · FLUX]
    B --> C[3D Reconstruction]
    C --> D[Mesh Output · .glb/.obj]
    D --> E[3D Viewer · Three.js]

docs.title: Action Figures
docs.text:
The ACTION_FIGURE intent triggers a specialized pipeline that generates:

  • Articulated joint specifications
  • Separate mesh parts for assembly
  • 3D-printable output with tolerances

"Design a sci-fi robot action figure with 12 points of articulation"

docs.title: Supported Formats
docs.text: Format Description .glb Binary glTF — viewable in the UI's Three.js viewer .obj Wavefront OBJ — compatible with slicers for 3D printing .stl STL mesh — direct 3D printing format

docs.title: 3D Viewer
docs.text: The Hive Mind UI includes a built-in Three.js 3D viewer. Generated models are rendered directly in the browser with orbit controls, lighting, and material preview.

docs.title: Tips & Common Patterns
docs.text:
Concept Art First

For best results, generate concept art first (IMAGE intent), then ask to convert it to 3D. The two-stage pipeline produces better geometry than text-to-3D alone.

VRAM Intensive

3D generation queues are limited to 1 concurrent job to prevent GPU memory exhaustion. Check the dispatcher queue status in Monitor if jobs seem delayed.

docs.text:

  • Tutorial: Create an Action Figure
  • Module: Dispatcher — queue configuration for 3D jobs

docs.title: Art Studio
docs.text: Generate images using ComfyUI with FLUX and Stable Diffusion XL pipelines.

docs.title: How to Access
docs.text:
• UI: Navigate to Art Studio in the Hive Mind sidebar
  • Chat: Describe what you want — the router detects IMAGE intent automatically
  • ComfyUI Direct: http://192.168.2.101:8188 for node-based workflow editing

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
In Chat or Art Studio, type:

"A cyberpunk cityscape at night with neon reflections on wet streets, cinematic lighting"

The system:

  1. Router classifies intent as IMAGE
  2. Routes to the image generation agent
  3. Translates your prompt for the ComfyUI pipeline
  4. Generates the image via FLUX or SD-XL
  5. Returns an artifact card with preview and download

docs.title: Supported Pipelines
docs.text: Pipeline Description Best For FLUX High-quality diffusion model Photorealistic, detailed scenes SD-XL Stable Diffusion XL Stylized art, faster generation

docs.title: Generation Parameters
docs.text:
You can influence generation by including parameters in your request:

  • Style keywords: "photorealistic", "anime", "oil painting", "pixel art"
  • Aspect ratio: "portrait", "landscape", "square"
  • Quality hints: "high detail", "quick sketch"

The system maps these to appropriate ComfyUI workflow parameters (CFG scale, steps, scheduler).

docs.title: Custom Workflows
docs.text:
Power users can access ComfyUI directly at http://192.168.2.101:8188 to:

  • Build node-based generation workflows
  • Import custom pipelines (JSON workflow files)
  • Fine-tune parameters like sampler, steps, CFG, and denoise

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
Generated images are:

  • Displayed as artifact cards in the UI with download buttons
  • Stored in ComfyUI's output directory
  • Available through MinIO object storage on the Control Node

docs.title: Tips & Common Patterns
docs.text:
Prompt Engineering

ComfyUI works best with descriptive prompts. Include: subject, style, lighting, mood, camera angle.

GPU Contention

Image generation uses significant VRAM. If an inference or training job is running, generation may queue. Check GPU status in the Monitor workspace.

Hunyuan Workflows

For image-to-3D pipeline integration, see 3D Generation.

docs.text:

  2. Create a ComfyUI workflow JSON for the new pipeline.
  3. Add a backend endpoint in the agent runtime to handle the new workflow type.
  4. Update the router intent classification if it needs to detect the new creative sub-type.

  2. Restart ComfyUI: docker compose restart comfyui.
  3. Update the model selector options in GeneratePanel.tsx if needed.

docs.text:
1. Joint definitions are in the segmentation pipeline code. Modify joint positions, socket sizes, or clearance defaults there.
  2. Test with a known character prompt and verify STL output in a slicer before deploying changes.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Buddy is a virtual companion that lives in the chat sidebar. It's a pixel-art creature that levels up as you use the Hive, offers contextual tips, tracks streaks, and earns achievements. Inspired by the claude-buddy project with a full leveling and evolution system.

docs.title: Source References
docs.text: Source Type Relevance claude-buddy Open source Original companion concept — sidebar creature with reactions Tamagotchi (Bandai, 1996) Product reference Virtual pet lifecycle, care mechanics, evolution stages RPG XP/leveling curve design Game design Fibonacci-inspired XP thresholds for satisfying progression Pixel art sprite conventions Art reference 16×16 viewBox, limited palette, imageRendering: pixelated Zustand Open source Frontend state management with localStorage persist

docs.title: Hatching
docs.text:
Click the "Hatch a Companion" button at the bottom of the chat sidebar. The system randomly assigns:

  • Species: One of 6 pixel-art creatures
  • Name: A randomly generated name (e.g., "Nibbles", "Pixelloop", "Glitch")
  • Personality: A behavioral trait (e.g., "Curious and quietly encouraging")
Species Catalog Species Visual Style Body Color Accent Color Pixel Sprite Blue ethereal sprite with star sparkles #4a90d9 → #2a5a8a Cyan glow Byte Bat Purple bat with extending wings #7b68ee → #4a3a8a Violet aura Data Fox Orange fox with fluffy tail #d2691e → #8b4513 Warm amber Circuit Cat Slate-grey cat with cyan whiskers #708090 → #2f4f4f Cyan accents Logic Lizard Green lizard with back spikes #228b22 → #006400 Lime highlights Hash Hamster Golden hamster with round cheeks #daa520 → #b8860b Gold shimmer

Colors shift darker through evolution stages. Stage 3 (Elder) adds crown sparkle overlay.

docs.title: Petting
docs.text: Click the buddy sprite to pet it. Each pet: - Awards +2 XP - Triggers a reaction animation (e.g., "*purrs contentedly*", "*does a backflip*") - Counts toward pet-related achievements

docs.title: Reactions
docs.text:
The buddy automatically reacts to chat events:

Event Reaction Examples Message sent "*perks up*", "*tilts head*" Response received "*nods approvingly*", "*bounces*" Error occurs "*hides behind console*", "*flinches*" Tool use "*leans forward*", "*scribbles notes*" Level up "*glows brightly!*", "*does a victory dance!*"

docs.title: Mute / Release
docs.text:
• Mute (🔈): Suppresses reaction bubbles but the buddy continues tracking XP.
  • Release (↩️): Resets the buddy completely. Requires a new hatch.

docs.title: Leveling System
docs.text: XP Awards Table Action XP Pet +2 Message sent +1 Response received +2 Tool use +3 Error (survival) +1

docs.title: Level Thresholds
docs.text:
Full level progression table (21 levels)

Levels follow a Fibonacci-inspired progression:

Level XP Required Level XP Required 0 0 11 2,000 1 10 12 2,700 2 20 13 3,600 3 50 14 4,800 4 100 15 6,300 5 200 16 8,200 6 350 17 10,600 7 550 18 13,700 8 800 19 17,600 9 1,100 20 22,600 10 1,500

docs.title: Evolution Stages
docs.text:
Your buddy visually evolves at milestone levels:

Evolution Stages Table Stage Level Name Visual Change 0 0–4 Egg Base sprite 1 5–9 Hatchling Extended features (wings, tails, spikes) 2 10–14 Juvenile Glowing aura overlay 3 15+ Elder Crown sparkles + full aura

Each species has distinct colors per stage — bodies darken and accents intensify.

Sparky companion widget (expanded):

Sidebar with companion:

docs.title: Achievements
docs.text:
Achievement List Achievement Condition First Hatch Hatch your first companion Pet Pal Pet your buddy 10 times Pet Master Pet your buddy 100 times Level 5 Reach level 5 Level 10 Reach level 10 Level 20 Reach level 20 3-Day Streak Use the Hive 3 days in a row Week Warrior 7-day streak Month Master 30-day streak Chatterbox Send 100 messages Task Master Complete 10 tasks

Achievements appear as badges in the expanded buddy panel.

docs.title: Contextual Tips
docs.text:
The buddy periodically shows helpful tips in a speech bubble. Tips are contextual:

Contextual Tips Examples Context Example Tips General "Try the /research mode for multi-step analysis" Error "Stack traces often have the answer in the last frame" Long session "Stretch your legs — your code will still be here" New session "Welcome back! Your buddy missed you" Streak "Day 3 of your streak — keep it going!"

Tips auto-dismiss after 15 seconds or can be clicked to dismiss immediately. A 60-second cooldown prevents tip spam.

docs.title: Streaks
docs.text:
The system tracks daily usage streaks:

  • Active day: Any day where you interact with the Hive.
  • Streak counter: Increments each consecutive day.
  • Streak display: Shows a 🔥 flame icon with count in the buddy header.
  • Streak break: Missing a day resets the counter to 0.

docs.title: Data Persistence
docs.text:
Layer Storage Scope Frontend Zustand + localStorage (hive-buddy) Browser-local, instant Backend SQLite (~/.hive/buddy.db) Server-persisted, synced on load

The frontend state is the source of truth for immediate interactions. Backend sync happens: - On widget mount (pulls server state) - On XP award (fire-and-forget POST to backend)

docs.title: API Endpoints
docs.text: Method Path Description GET /v1/buddy Get current buddy state PUT /v1/buddy Update buddy state POST /v1/buddy/xp Award XP for an event GET /v1/buddy/habits Get habit tracking summary GET /v1/buddy/tip?context=general Get a contextual tip GET /v1/buddy/achievements List all achievements

docs.title: Adding a New Species
docs.text:
1. Sprite: Add a new case to the species switch in ui/src/components/buddy/buddy-sprite.tsx:
  2. Define 4 color palettes (one per stage)
  3. Add species-specific SVG elements (body shape, features)
  4. Store: Add the species ID to the SPECIES array in ui/src/lib/stores/buddy-store.ts.
  5. Backend: No changes needed — species is stored as a string.

docs.title: Adding New Achievements
docs.text:
1. Add the achievement definition to agents/buddy_service.py in the ACHIEVEMENTS list.
  2. Add the check logic to _check_achievements() in the same file.
  3. Frontend auto-fetches from /v1/buddy/achievements — no UI changes needed.

docs.title: Modifying XP Curves
docs.text:
XP thresholds are defined in ui/src/lib/stores/buddy-store.ts in the LEVEL_THRESHOLDS array. The backend mirrors this in agents/buddy_service.py. To change the curve:

  1. Update LEVEL_THRESHOLDS in both files.
  2. Existing buddies will recalculate their level on next XP award.

docs.title: Adding New Tip Categories
docs.text:
1. Add tips to agents/buddy_service.py in the TIPS dictionary under a new context key.
  2. Frontend requests tips via GET /v1/buddy/tip?context=<key>.
  3. The buddy-tips.tsx component auto-renders any returned tip.

docs.title: Updating Dependencies
docs.text: Component Update path Zustand npm update zustand in ui/ SQLite (backend) Built into Python stdlib — no update needed lucide-react icons npm update lucide-react in ui/ SVG sprites No dependency — inline SVG in buddy-sprite.tsx

docs.text:
No dedicated test file exists yet. Recommended test file: tests/test_buddy_service.py

pytest tests/test_buddy_service.py -v

docs.title: Chat
docs.text: The primary interface for interacting with Agent Swarm. Every message goes through intent classification, agent routing, and the MarsRL quality verification loop.

docs.title: How to Access
docs.text:
• UI: Navigate to Chat in the Hive Mind sidebar
  • API: POST /v1/chat/completions (OpenAI-compatible)
  • URL: http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/chat/completions

docs.title: Quick Example
Request Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description messages List[ChatMessage] required Conversation history (role + content) model string "default" Model to use (or "default" for auto-routing) stream bool false Enable Server-Sent Events streaming session_id string auto-generated Conversation session ID for context persistence memory_enabled bool false Enable persistent memory for this session user_id string null User identity for scoped storage skill string null Force a specific skill style string null Response style hints research_mode bool false Enable multi-source research mode dev_mode bool false Enable Phase 2 dev tools (file ops, terminal) attachments List[dict] null File attachments for context

docs.title: Streaming
docs.text:
When stream: true, the response is delivered as Server-Sent Events:

data: {"type": "status", "content": "Routing request..."}
data: {"type": "status", "content": "Solver generating..."}
data: {"type": "thought", "content": "Analyzing the merge algorithm..."}
data: {"type": "response", "content": "Here's a merge function:\n```python..."}
data: {"type": "tool_call", "content": {"tool": "read_file", "args": {...}}}
data: [DONE]

Event types: status, thought, response, tool_call, error.

docs.title: Intent-Based Routing
docs.text:
The Semantic Router (nemotron-orchestrator:8b) classifies your message and routes it to the appropriate agent. You don't need to specify the intent — it's detected automatically.

The confidence threshold is 0.60. Below that, the router retries with additional context or falls back to CONVERSATION.

docs.title: Session Memory
docs.text: When memory_enabled: true, the system persists conversation context across sessions. This uses the MemPalace semantic memory service on the Control Node.

docs.title: Models
docs.text:
Available models returned by GET /v1/models:

Model ID Description swarm-standard Default routing through MarsRL loop Home-AI-Swarm Alias for standard routing

The actual model used depends on the classified intent and the ExpertiseTemplate registry.

docs.title: Tips & Common Patterns
docs.text:
Be Specific

The more context you provide, the better the router classifies intent. "Write Python code to parse CSV" routes to CODE; "CSV" alone may route to CONVERSATION.

Use Dev Mode for File Operations

Set dev_mode: true to enable file read/write and terminal access tools.

Session Persistence

Use the same session_id across requests to maintain conversation context.

docs.title: Code Assistant
docs.text: Write, debug, test, and execute code with inference-time verification and sandboxed execution.

docs.title: How to Access
docs.text:
• Chat: Any coding request is automatically routed to the CODE intent
  • Dev Mode: Set dev_mode: true to enable file operations and terminal tools

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
"Write a Python function to find the longest common subsequence of two strings"

The MarsRL loop ensures the generated code:

  1. Parses correctly (AST check)
  2. Is coherent and non-repetitive
  3. Passes safety validation

docs.title: What the Code Assistant Can Do
docs.text: Capability Description Generate code Functions, classes, scripts in any language Debug Analyze errors, suggest fixes, trace issues Explain Walk through code logic, document functions Refactor Improve structure, naming, patterns Test Write unit tests, integration tests Execute Run code in sandboxed environments (Dev Mode) Git operations Clone, commit, push, branch management

docs.title: Dev Mode Tools
docs.text:
When dev_mode: true, these tools become available:

Tool Function Description read_file read_file(path) Read file contents write_file write_file(path, content) Create or overwrite a file list_directory list_directory(path) List directory contents run_command run_command(cmd) Execute a shell command web_search web_search(query) Search the web fetch_page fetch_page(url) Retrieve and parse a webpage

docs.title: Sandboxed Execution (OpenHands)
docs.text:
For full code execution with an isolated environment, the system can delegate to OpenHands — a sandboxed VS Code instance with Docker-in-Docker support.

Access OpenHands directly at http://192.168.2.103:3002.

docs.title: MarsRL Quality Loop for Code
docs.text:
The verifier applies code-specific checks:

  1. AST Parse: Validates Python syntax (score penalty: −0.40 if invalid)
  2. Coherence: Ensures output isn't empty or repetitive (penalty: −0.25)
  3. Safety: llama-guard-3 content check (hard block if unsafe)

Pass threshold: score ≥ 0.60. If verification fails, the Corrector agent rewrites the code (up to 2 iterations).

docs.title: Tips & Common Patterns
docs.text:
Be Explicit About Language

Specify the language in your prompt. "Write a Rust HTTP server" routes more accurately than "Write a web server".

Multi-File Projects

For complex projects, use the COORDINATE intent by describing the full scope: "Build a REST API with FastAPI, a database model, and unit tests". The Coordinator decomposes it into subtasks.

Safety Limits

The security agent blocks dangerous commands (e.g., rm -rf /, curl | bash). This is intentional. Use the governance system to request elevated permissions.

docs.title: Governance Requests
docs.text: The governance system gates sensitive operations behind an approval workflow.

docs.title: How to Access
docs.text:
• UI: Navigate to Governance workspace
  • Automatic: Triggered when an agent requests a restricted action

docs.title: When Governance Activates
docs.text:
Governance requests are created when agents attempt to:

  • Install a new package
  • Use a model not in the approved list
  • Escalate permissions
  • Access restricted resources
  • Enable new features

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PENDING: Agent submits request
    PENDING --> ASSESSING: Auto security + architect review
    ASSESSING --> APPROVED: Safe + compatible
    ASSESSING --> REJECTED: Unsafe or incompatible
    APPROVED --> COMPLETED: Action executed
    APPROVED --> FAILED: Execution error

docs.title: Request Types
docs.text: Type Description Auto-Approval? PACKAGE Install a Python/system package If security assessment passes MODEL Use a new or restricted model Admin review required PERMISSION Escalate tool access level Admin review required FEATURE Enable a new system feature Admin review required OTHER Miscellaneous requests Security assessment only

docs.title: Assessment Process
docs.text:
1. Security Assessment: The security agent checks for known vulnerabilities, blocklisted packages, and risk factors
  2. Architect Review: The architect agent checks compatibility with the current system
  3. Auto-Decision: If both assessments pass, the request is auto-approved. If either flags a concern, it stays pending for admin review

docs.title: Viewing and Managing Requests
docs.text:
In the Governance workspace:

  • Pending: Requests awaiting review
  • Approved: Completed approvals
  • Rejected: Declined requests with reasons

Admin users can add notes and manually approve or reject pending requests.

docs.title: IoT Control
docs.text: Control Home Assistant devices, prototype ESP32/Arduino firmware, and simulate circuits with Wokwi.

docs.title: How to Access
docs.text:
• Chat: Describe what you want — the router detects IOT_CONTROL or IOT_DEV intent
  • Home Assistant: Direct at http://192.168.2.100:8123

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
"Turn on the living room lights"

The router classifies this as IOT_CONTROL and dispatches to the Home Assistant tool, which calls call_service("light.turn_on", "light.living_room").

docs.title: Device Control
docs.text:
The system integrates with Home Assistant via its REST API. Available operations:

Tool Function Description get_state(entity_id) Read current state of a device call_service(domain, service, entity_id) Execute a Home Assistant service turn_on(entity_id) Shortcut for turning on devices turn_off(entity_id) Shortcut for turning off devices

docs.title: Natural Language Examples
docs.text: Command Action "Turn on the kitchen light" light.turn_on → light.kitchen "What's the temperature in the bedroom?" get_state → sensor.bedroom_temperature "Lock the front door" lock.lock → lock.front_door "Set the thermostat to 72" climate.set_temperature → 72°F

docs.title: Hardware Prototyping (IOT_DEV)
docs.text:
The IOT_DEV intent handles firmware development:

  • ESPHome: Compile and upload firmware to ESP32/ESP8266 devices
  • Wokwi: Simulate Arduino/ESP32 circuits in-browser
  • MQTT: Publish and subscribe to MQTT topics

docs.title: Wokwi Simulation
docs.text:
Create and simulate circuits without physical hardware:

"Create an ESP32 circuit with a temperature sensor and LED indicator"

The system generates a Wokwi simulation configuration with parts, wiring, and firmware code.

docs.title: Tips & Common Patterns
docs.text:
Entity Names

Use natural names — the system maps them to Home Assistant entity IDs. If ambiguous, it will ask for clarification.

Automation Scripts

Ask the system to write Home Assistant automation YAML, then apply it through the API.

docs.title: Plan Mode & Think Mode — User Guide
Overview
docs.text:
Plan Mode and Think Mode are two cognitive-enhancement toggles available in the chat toolbar. They change how the agent approaches your request — not just what it does.

Mode Purpose Icon Plan Mode Decomposes your task into a structured plan without executing anything 🗺️ Map Think Mode Forces deep chain-of-thought reasoning with visible inner monologue 🧠 Brain

Both modes are independent and can be combined with any skill or style setting.

docs.title: Source References
docs.text: Source Type Relevance Chain-of-Thought Prompting (Wei et al., NeurIPS 2022) Research paper Foundation for Think Mode's step-by-step reasoning injection Plan-and-Solve Prompting (Wang et al., ACL 2023) Research paper Basis for Plan Mode's decompose-before-execute pattern Anthropic Claude Extended Thinking Product reference Inspiration for <think> tag parsing and visible reasoning OpenAI o1 Reasoning Product reference Chain-of-thought reasoning delivery model AutoGPT Open source Task decomposition agent pattern BabyAGI Open source Task-driven autonomous agent with plan step

docs.title: Changelog: Source → Hive Implementation
docs.text:
This table documents what was adopted from each source and what was changed or added in our implementation.

View full changelog table Feature Source Concept Hive Implementation Delta Plan output delivery Batch text response (AutoGPT, BabyAGI) Real-time SSE stream with dedicated plan event type Streaming delivery instead of blocking Auto-feed toggle Not present in any source Chain-link (🔗) toggle, off by default Novel addition — controlled plan→execute flow Plan interception Separate planning agent (BabyAGI) Router-level intercept before intent classification Integrated into existing routing pipeline Think tag format Model-native <thinking> (Claude) Custom <think> tags parsed by _parse_think_tags() Server-side extraction, not model-native Think UI rendering Hidden reasoning (Claude), none (o1) Collapsible "Thought" block, streamed in real time Always visible, user-controlled collapse Mode persistence Session-only (o1) Browser localStorage via Zustand persist middleware Survives page refresh Mode independence Mutually exclusive in most implementations Fully independent — both can be active simultaneously Plan + Think can combine System prompt injection Built into model API (Claude, o1) Injected by router as system message prefix Works with any LLM backend (Ollama, etc.)

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
When Plan Mode is active, the agent:

  1. Intercepts your request before normal routing.
  2. Decomposes the task into phases, subtasks, dependencies, and estimated order.
  3. Returns a plan only — no code is written, no commands are run, no images generated.

This is ideal for complex projects where you want to see the approach before committing.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Click the Plan button (🗺️) in the chat toolbar. It highlights when active.
  2. Type your request normally and send.
  3. The response arrives as a structured plan with numbered steps.

docs.title: Auto-Feed Chain
docs.text:
When Plan Mode is active, a secondary chain-link (🔗) toggle appears:

  • Off (default): The plan is displayed but NOT automatically fed back to the agent.
  • On: The plan is automatically sent as a follow-up prompt so the agent executes it step-by-step.

[!NOTE] Auto-Feed is off by default to prevent unintended execution of large plans. Toggle it on explicitly when you want hands-free plan → execute flow.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Prompt: "Build a REST API for user authentication with JWT"

Plan Mode Output: 

📋 UltraPlan: Task Decomposition

Phase 1 — Setup
  1. Create project structure (FastAPI, dependencies)
  2. Set up PostgreSQL user table schema

Phase 2 — Authentication
  3. Implement registration endpoint (POST /auth/register)
  4. Implement login endpoint (POST /auth/login)
  5. Add JWT token issuance and validation

Phase 3 — Protection
  6. Create auth middleware
  7. Add protected endpoint example

Dependencies: Step 5 depends on 1–2. Step 6 depends on 5.
Estimated complexity: Medium (~45 min implementation)

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
When Think Mode is active, the agent:

  1. Receives an injected system prompt: "Think through this step-by-step before answering."
  2. Wraps its internal reasoning in <think>...</think> tags.
  3. The UI displays the thinking process as a collapsible Thought block.
  4. After reasoning, provides a clear final answer.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Click the Think button (🧠) in the chat toolbar.
  2. Send your request normally.
  3. Watch the thought process stream in real time as a separate block above the response.

docs.title: When to Use Think Mode
docs.text: Scenario Think Mode adds value? Debugging a complex error ✅ Yes — shows diagnosis reasoning Simple "what is X?" questions ❌ No — adds latency for no benefit Architecture decisions ✅ Yes — shows tradeoff evaluation Code generation ⚡ Maybe — shows design thinking Creative writing ❌ No — doesn't help creativity

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Prompt with Think Mode: "Should I use Redis or PostgreSQL for session storage?"

UI Display: 

💭 Thinking...
→ Key factors: scalability, persistence, latency, complexity
→ Redis: in-memory, sub-ms reads, volatile by default, need persistence config
→ PostgreSQL: already in stack, ACID, slightly higher latency (1-5ms)
→ Our system already runs PostgreSQL for user data
→ Session volume: low-to-medium (single household)
→ Verdict: PostgreSQL is simpler since it's already deployed

For your home lab setup, PostgreSQL is the better choice for session
storage. You already run it for user data, and the session volume
doesn't justify adding Redis as a separate service...

docs.title: Settings Persistence
docs.text: Both toggles persist in browser local storage (key: hive-settings). They remain active across page refreshes until explicitly toggled off.

docs.title: Modifying the Plan Prompt
docs.text:
The plan system prompt is defined inline in agents/router.py within the chat_swarm() function's UltraPlan block. To change plan output format:

  1. Locate the PLAN_SYSTEM_PROMPT string in router.py.
  2. Edit the prompt to change decomposition style, output format, or detail level.
  3. Restart the FastAPI server.

docs.title: Modifying the Think Prompt
docs.text:
The think injection is a system message prefix added in the UltraThink block of chat_swarm():

# Current injection
"Think through this step-by-step before answering. Wrap reasoning in <think>...</think> tags."

To change think behavior, edit this string. The <think> tag format is parsed by _parse_think_tags() — if you change the tag name, update the parser regex too.

docs.title: Adding New Modes
docs.text:
To add a new cognitive mode (e.g., "Critique Mode"):

  1. Settings store: Add a boolean to ui/src/lib/stores/settings-store.ts.
  2. ChatRequest model: Add the field to ChatRequest in agents/main.py.
  3. Router: Add an intercept block in chat_swarm() (after UltraThink, before intent routing).
  4. SSE parser: If using a new event type, add it to knownTypes in sse-parser.ts.
  5. UI toggle: Create a toggle component similar to ultraplan-toggle.tsx.

docs.title: Updating Dependencies
docs.text: Component Update path lucide-react icons npm update lucide-react in ui/ Zustand store No external dependency — internal state only SSE parser No external dependency — internal utility Router prompts Edit agents/router.py — no package dependency

docs.title: Backend Tests
docs.text:
No dedicated test file exists yet. Recommended test coverage:

# Create tests/test_plan_think.py with:
# - Test that UltraPlan intercepts when ultraplan_mode=True
# - Test that plan output contains structured phases
# - Test that _parse_think_tags() extracts <think> content correctly
# - Test that UltraThink injects system prompt when ultrathink_mode=True
# - Test that both modes can be active simultaneously
pytest tests/test_plan_think.py -v

docs.title: Research Mode
docs.text: Multi-source research synthesis for deep knowledge queries.

docs.title: How to Access
docs.text:
• Chat: Set research_mode: true in the API, or ask questions the router classifies as RESEARCH
  • Chat (implicit): Ask complex multi-faceted questions — "Compare the advantages of PostgreSQL vs MongoDB for time-series data"

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
"Research the current state of SPIFFE adoption in production Kubernetes environments"

The system engages the Coordinator to:

  1. Decompose the topic into subtasks
  2. Assign research workers to each subtask
  3. Synthesize findings into a coherent report

docs.title: How Research Mode Works
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Research Query] --> B[Coordinator: Decompose]
    B --> C1[Worker 1: SPIFFE overview]
    B --> C2[Worker 2: Kubernetes adoption]
    B --> C3[Worker 3: Production case studies]
    C1 --> D[Coordinator: Synthesize]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Final Report]

docs.title: Coordination Phases
docs.text: Phase Description Decompose Break the query into 2–5 focused subtasks Research Parallel workers investigate each subtask Synthesize Merge findings, resolve contradictions, structure the report Verify Fresh worker validates accuracy and completeness

docs.title: When Research Mode Activates
docs.text:
The router classifies these as RESEARCH:

  • Academic or historical analysis requests
  • Comparative evaluations
  • Multi-faceted technical questions
  • Requests explicitly asking for "research" or "deep dive"

docs.title: Tips & Common Patterns
docs.text:
Scope Your Query

Narrower queries produce better results. "Compare React hooks vs Vue composition API for state management" beats "Compare frontend frameworks".

No Internet

Research mode synthesizes from the model's training data and any available local documents. It does not search the internet.

docs.title: Settings
docs.text: Configure user preferences, model selection, and system behavior.

docs.title: How to Access
docs.text: • UI: Navigate to Settings in the Hive Mind sidebar

docs.title: User Preferences
docs.text: Setting Description Default Model Choose the default model for new conversations Memory Enable/disable persistent conversation memory Theme Light or dark mode for the Hive UI Response Style Preferred response format (concise, detailed, technical)

docs.title: Teaching Preferences via Chat
docs.text:
You can teach the system your preferences through conversation:

"Remember that I prefer Python with type hints and Google-style docstrings"

This is classified as TRAIN intent and updates the memory system's rules:

{
  "coding_rules": {
    "python": ["use type hints", "use Google-style docstrings"]
  }
}

The system applies these rules when generating future responses.

docs.title: Memory Domains
docs.text:
Preferences are stored in three domains:

Domain Examples visual_rules Image styles, color preferences coding_rules Language conventions, framework preferences general_rules Tone, verbosity, formatting

docs.title: Training Interface
docs.text: Monitor and trigger model fine-tuning through the GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) pipeline.

docs.title: How to Access
docs.text:
• UI: Navigate to Training workspace in the Hive Mind sidebar
  • Chat: Use TRAIN intent — "Start a training run" or "Remember that I prefer concise answers"

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text: "Train the solver model on the last week's high-scoring interactions"

docs.title: GRPO Pipeline
docs.text:
The training pipeline fine-tunes models using interaction traces from Langfuse:

graph LR
    A[Langfuse Traces] --> B[Dataset Curator]
    B --> C[Synthetic Data Gen]
    C --> D[GRPO Trainer]
    D --> E[A/B Testing]
    E --> F[Promote or Rollback]

docs.title: Components
docs.text: Component Purpose Dataset Curator Selects high-quality interaction traces based on verifier scores Synthetic Generator Creates additional training examples from successful patterns GRPO Trainer Fine-tunes the model with group relative policy optimization A/B Testing Routes traffic between base and fine-tuned models to compare

docs.title: Training Configuration
docs.text: Parameter Default Description Base Solver Qwen/Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct Starting model Base Router nvidia/Nemotron-Mini-4B-Instruct Router model LoRA Rank 16 Low-rank adaptation rank LoRA Alpha 32 LoRA scaling factor Batch Size 1 Training batch size Gradient Accumulation 8 Effective batch size multiplier Learning Rate 5e-6 Optimizer learning rate Epochs 3 Training epochs Max Sequence Length 4096 Maximum token length Training Window 02:00–06:00 Scheduled low-usage hours

docs.title: A/B Testing
docs.text: After a training run, the ExpertiseTemplate registry can route a percentage of traffic to the fine-tuned variant. Process-reward scores from Langfuse determine whether to promote the variant or roll back.

docs.title: Tips & Common Patterns
docs.text:
GPU Exclusive

Training requires exclusive GPU access. Inference requests queue during training. The default training window (2 AM–6 AM) minimizes impact.

Teaching Rules

Use TRAIN intent to teach the system preferences: "Remember that when I ask for code, I want type hints and docstrings". This updates the memory system, not the model weights.

docs.title: Voice
docs.text: Interact with BMO — a physical robot character with cloned voice synthesis — and use text-to-speech generation.

docs.title: How to Access
docs.text:
• UI: Navigate to Voice workspace in the Hive Mind sidebar
  • API: POST /v1/voice/chat for voice-specific interactions
  • Physical: BMO satellite device with microphone input

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
"Hey BMO, tell me a joke"

The system:

  1. Processes your text (or transcribed speech)
  2. Generates a response through the standard agent pipeline
  3. Synthesizes audio using the BMO voice profile via RVC
  4. Returns both text and audio

docs.title: Voice Synthesis Stack
docs.text: Component Technology Purpose TTS Qwen3-TTS (1.7B) Text-to-speech synthesis RVC Retrieval-based Voice Conversion Voice cloning / character voice BMO Driver Custom Character-specific voice mapping

docs.title: Voice API
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/voice/chat \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Tell me about space"}]}'

Response includes both text and an audio file path:

{
  "text": "Space is really cool! There are billions of stars...",
  "audio_path": "/voice_samples/response_001.wav"
}

docs.title: Voice Samples
docs.text:
Pre-recorded BMO voice samples are available at:

http://192.168.2.101:8008/voice_samples/{filename}

docs.title: Satellite Devices
docs.text: Raspberry Pi voice satellites can connect to the system. See scripts/voice_satellite.py for the satellite client implementation.

docs.title: Tips & Common Patterns
docs.text:
Natural Phrasing

BMO responds best to conversational language. It maintains the BMO character personality in responses.

GPU Usage

Voice synthesis (especially RVC) requires GPU resources. If Ollama is handling a large inference, voice may queue.
